Question:
The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908 came into force on which date?
(A) First day of January, 1909
(B) First day of January, 1908
(C) First day of July, 1908
(D) First day of July, 1909

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 1
Possible Sections.Full Text: This Act may be cited as the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908. It shall come into force on the first day of January, 1909. It extends to the whole of Bangladesh.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 2
Possible Sections.Full Text: In this Act, unless there is anything repugnant in the subject or context,- "Code" includes rules; "decree" means the formal expression of an adjudication...

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 3
Possible Sections.Full Text: For the purposes of this Code, the District Court is subordinate to the High Court Division, and every Civil Court of a grade inferior to that of a District Court...

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 4
Possible Sections.Full Text: In the absence of any specific provision to the contrary, nothing in this Code shall be deemed to limit or otherwise affect any special law now in force...

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 5
Possible Sections.Full Text: Where any Revenue Courts are governed by the provisions of this Code in those matters of procedure upon which any special enactment applicable to them is silent...

Relevant Section: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 1
Section Text: This Act may be cited as the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908. It shall come into force on the first day of January, 1909. It extends to the whole of Bangladesh.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Determining the official commencement date of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908.

Rule.Section 1: This section provides the short title, extent, and specific date of commencement for the Act.
Rule.Section 2: This provision contains definitions for terms used throughout the Code to ensure contextual clarity.
Rule.Section 3: This section establishes the hierarchy and subordination of various civil courts within the judicial system.
Rule.Section 4: This provision acts as a savings clause for special laws and jurisdictions currently in force.
Rule.Section 5: This section regulates the application of the Code's procedural rules to specialized Revenue Courts.

Application.Section 1: Subsection (2) explicitly mandates that the Act shall come into force on January 1, 1909.
Application.Section 2: This section defines terms like decree and judgment but does not specify any commencement dates.
Application.Section 3: The text focuses on the administrative subordination of courts rather than the Act's operational timeline.
Application.Section 4: Savings clauses preserve existing powers but do not dictate when the new Code takes effect.
Application.Section 5: This applies specifically to Revenue Courts and does not govern the general commencement of the Act.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, section 1, Subsection (2), the Act officially came into force on the first day of January, 1909. While the Act was passed in 1908, its operational effect was deferred until the start of the following year. Therefore, (A) First day of January, 1909 is correct.

Question:
Under the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, 'decree' includes which of the following?
(A) An order of dismissal for default
(B) A statement given by the Judge of the grounds of an order
(C) Any adjudication from which an appeal lies as an appeal from an order
(D) The rejection of a plaint

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 2
Possible Sections.Full Text: "decree" means the formal expression of an adjudication which, so far as regards the Court expressing it, conclusively determines the rights of the parties...

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 9
Possible Sections.Full Text: The Courts shall (subject to the provisions herein contained) have jurisdiction to try all suits of a civil nature excepting suits of which their cognizance is either expressly or impliedly barred.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 14
Possible Sections.Full Text: The Court shall presume, upon the production of any document purporting to be a certified copy of a foreign judgment, that such judgment was pronounced by a Court of competent jurisdiction...

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 33
Possible Sections.Full Text: The Court, after the case has been heard, shall pronounce judgment, and on such judgment a decree shall follow.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 2(14)
Possible Sections.Full Text: "order" means the formal expression of any decision of a Civil Court which is not a decree.

Relevant Section: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 2
Section Text: "decree" means the formal expression of an adjudication which, so far as regards the Court expressing it, conclusively determines the rights of the parties with regard to all or any of the matters in controversy in the suit and may be either preliminary or final. It shall be deemed to include the rejection of a plaint and the determination of any question within section 144, but shall not include- (a) any adjudication from which an appeal lies as an appeal from an order, or (b) any order of dismissal for default.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Identifying the statutory inclusions and exclusions in the definition of a 'decree'.

Rule.Section 2: This section defines a decree as a formal adjudication conclusively determining rights, specifically including plaint rejections.
Rule.Section 9: This provision establishes the general jurisdiction of civil courts to try all suits of a civil nature.
Rule.Section 14: This section deals with the legal presumption of the competence of foreign courts issuing judgments.
Rule.Section 33: This section describes the procedural sequence where a decree follows the formal pronouncement of a judgment.
Rule.Section 2(14): This specific subsection distinguishes an 'order' as a formal decision that does not qualify as a decree.

Application.Section 2: Subsection (2) explicitly states that the rejection of a plaint is deemed to be included within the definition of a decree.
Application.Section 9: The scope of jurisdiction does not define or list the components of a court's formal adjudication.
Application.Section 14: Presumptions about foreign judgments are irrelevant to the internal definition of a domestic decree.
Application.Section 33: While it mentions that decrees follow judgments, it does not detail the substantive content of what a decree includes.
Application.Section 2(14): This section defines an 'order' by what it is not, failing to provide the positive inclusions for a decree.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, section 2, Subsection (2), the term 'decree' is defined as a formal adjudication of rights. Crucially, the statute deems the rejection of a plaint to be a decree, while specifically excluding dismissal for default. Therefore, (D) The rejection of a plaint is correct.

Question:
A resides in Chittagong and beats B in Dhaka. Under Section 19 of the CPC, where can B sue A for compensation?
(A) Either in Dhaka or in Chittagong
(B) Only in Chittagong
(C) Only in a Court designated by the High Court Division
(D) Only in Dhaka

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 15
Possible Sections.Full Text: Every suit shall be instituted in the Court of the lowest grade competent to try it.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 16
Possible Sections.Full Text: Subject to the pecuniary or other limitations prescribed by any law, suits- for the recovery of immovable property... shall be instituted in the Court within the local limits of whose jurisdiction the property is situate.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 19
Possible Sections.Full Text: Where a suit is for compensation for wrong done to the person or to movable property... the suit may be instituted at the option of the plaintiff in either of the said Courts.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 20
Possible Sections.Full Text: Subject to the limitations aforesaid, every suit shall be instituted in a Court within the local limits of whose jurisdiction— the defendant... resides... or the cause of action... arises.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 21
Possible Sections.Full Text: No objection as to the place of suing shall be allowed by any appellate or revisional Court unless such objection was taken in the Court of first instance at the earliest possible opportunity...

Relevant Section: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 19
Section Text: Where a suit is for compensation for wrong done to the person or to movable property, if the wrong was done within the local limits of the jurisdiction of one Court and the defendant resides... within the local limits of the jurisdiction of another Court, the suit may be instituted at the option of the plaintiff in either of the said Courts. Illustration (a) A, residing in Chittagong beats B in Dhaka. B may sue A either in Dhaka or in Chittagong.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Determining the proper venue for a suit involving personal injury caused by a non-resident defendant.

Rule.Section 15: This rule requires all suits to be filed in the lowest grade court competent to adjudicate the matter.
Rule.Section 16: This provision mandates that suits concerning immovable property must be filed where the property is actually located.
Rule.Section 19: This section allows plaintiffs to choose between the venue where the wrong occurred or where the defendant resides.
Rule.Section 20: This general provision governs other suits, focusing on defendant residence or where the cause of action arises.
Rule.Section 21: This section restricts when objections to the place of suing can be raised in higher courts.

Application.Section 15: While establishing the grade of the court, it does not solve the geographical dispute between two districts.
Application.Section 16: Personal injury is not a wrong to immovable property, making this situs-based rule inapplicable to the facts.
Application.Section 19: This section specifically covers wrongs to persons, granting B the option to sue in Dhaka or Chittagong.
Application.Section 20: This section applies only 'subject to the limitations aforesaid,' meaning Section 19 takes precedence for personal wrongs.
Application.Section 21: This procedural rule regarding objections does not define the initial legal right to choose a specific forum.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, section 19, when a wrong is done to a person in one jurisdiction but the defendant resides in another, the plaintiff has the option to sue in either court. Therefore, (A) Either in Dhaka or in Chittagong is correct.

Question:
According to Section 80 of the CPC, what is the mandatory notice period before instituting a suit against the Government?
(A) One month
(B) Three months
(C) Six months
(D) Two months

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 79
Possible Sections.Full Text: In a suit by or against the Government the authority to be named as plaintiff or defendant, as the case may be, shall be Bangladesh.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 80
Possible Sections.Full Text: A suit may be instituted against the Government... after the expiration of two months next after notice in writing has been delivered...

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 81
Possible Sections.Full Text: In a suit instituted against a public officer... the defendant shall not be liable to arrest nor his property to attachment otherwise than in execution of a decree...

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 82
Possible Sections.Full Text: Where the decree is against the Government... a time shall be specified in the decree within which it shall be satisfied; and... Execution shall not be issued... unless it remains unsatisfied for the period of three months...

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 46
Possible Sections.Full Text: Upon the application of the decree-holder the Court which passed the decree may... issue a precept... Provided that no attachment under a precept shall continue for more than two months...

Relevant Section: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 80
Section Text: A suit may be instituted against the Government or against a public officer... after the expiration of two months next after notice in writing has been delivered to or left at the office of... a Secretary to the Government or the Collector of the District.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Identifying the required duration for a statutory notice before suing the state.

Rule.Section 79: This section dictates that 'Bangladesh' must be the named party in any suit involving the government.
Rule.Section 80: This provision mandates a two-month waiting period after delivering written notice before a suit can be filed.
Rule.Section 81: This section provides specific exemptions from arrest and personal appearance for public officers in official suits.
Rule.Section 82: This section deals with the post-decree phase, requiring a three-month waiting period before execution against the government.
Rule.Section 46: This provision limits the duration of an attachment under a court precept to a maximum of two months.

Application.Section 79: Naming the state as a party is a formal requirement but does not impose a time-based notice condition.
Application.Section 80: Subsection (1) explicitly requires that two months must expire after the notice is delivered before institution.
Application.Section 81: Protections against arrest are procedural safeguards that do not affect the pre-suit notice requirements.
Application.Section 82: The three-month period mentioned here applies to the satisfaction of a decree, not the filing of a suit.
Application.Section 46: The two-month limit for precepts is an execution-related timeframe unrelated to initiating suits against the state.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, section 80, Subsection (1), no suit shall be instituted against the Government or a public officer until the expiration of two months after a written notice has been delivered. Therefore, (D) Two months is correct.

Question:
Which explanation to Section 11 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908 deals with 'Constructive Res Judicata'?
(A) Explanation III
(B) Explanation IV
(C) Explanation II
(D) Explanation VI

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 10
Possible Sections.Full Text: No Court shall proceed with the trial of any suit in which the matter in issue is also directly and substantially in issue in a previously instituted suit...

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 11
Possible Sections.Full Text: No Court shall try any suit or issue in which the matter directly and substantially in issue has been directly and substantially in issue in a former suit...

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 12
Possible Sections.Full Text: Where a plaintiff is precluded by rules from instituting a further suit in respect of any particular cause of action, he shall not be entitled to institute a suit...

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 13
Possible Sections.Full Text: A foreign judgment shall be conclusive as to any matter thereby directly adjudicated upon between the same parties...

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 9
Possible Sections.Full Text: The Courts shall (subject to the provisions herein contained) have jurisdiction to try all suits of a civil nature...

Relevant Section: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 11
Section Text: Explanation IV.**—Any matter which might and ought to have been made ground of defence or attack in such former suit shall be deemed to have been a matter directly and substantially in issue in such suit.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Identifying the specific explanation within Section 11 defining constructive res judicata.

Rule.Section 10: This section governs the stay of suits during the pendency of a previously instituted matter.
Rule.Section 11: This provision bars the trial of issues already decided between the same parties in a former suit.
Rule.Section 12: This section prevents the filing of new suits when specific rules already preclude further action.
Rule.Section 13: This provision establishes when a foreign judgment is considered conclusive or non-conclusive between parties.
Rule.Section 9: This section grants civil courts the general authority to hear all suits of a civil nature.

Application.Section 10: The rule for staying trials does not address the finality of issues that ought to have been raised.
Application.Section 11: Explanation IV specifically deems matters that 'might and ought' to have been raised as having been decided.
Application.Section 12: Barring further suits based on procedural rules is distinct from the doctrine of constructive res judicata.
Application.Section 13: Foreign judgment conclusiveness focuses on jurisdictional and justice exceptions rather than omitted grounds of attack.
Application.Section 9: General jurisdiction principles do not define the specific explanations or limitations of the res judicata doctrine.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, section 11, Explanation IV, the doctrine of 'Constructive Res Judicata' is established. It mandates that any matter which might and ought to have been made a ground of defense or attack in a former suit is deemed decided. Therefore, (B) Explanation IV is correct.

Question:
A suit for the recovery of immovable property situated in Dhaka shall be instituted in which court?
(A) Any Court in Bangladesh at the option of the plaintiff
(B) The Court where the defendant resides
(C) The High Court Division only
(D) The Court within whose local limits the property is situate

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 15
Possible Sections.Full Text: Every suit shall be instituted in the Court of the lowest grade competent to try it.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 16
Possible Sections.Full Text: Subject to the pecuniary or other limitations prescribed by any law, suits- (a) for the recovery of immovable property... shall be instituted in the Court within the local limits of whose jurisdiction the property is situate.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 17
Possible Sections.Full Text: Where a suit is to obtain relief respecting... immovable property situate within the jurisdiction of different Courts, the suit may be instituted in any Court within the local limits of whose jurisdiction any portion of the property is situate.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 18
Possible Sections.Full Text: Where it is alleged to be uncertain within the local limits of the jurisdiction of which of two or more Courts any immovable property is situate...

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 20
Possible Sections.Full Text: Subject to the limitations aforesaid, every suit shall be instituted in a Court within the local limits of whose jurisdiction— the defendant... resides...

Relevant Section: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 16
Section Text: suits- (a) for the recovery of immovable property with or without rent or profits... shall be instituted in the Court within the local limits of whose jurisdiction the property is situate.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Determining the correct forum for a suit seeking recovery of immovable property.

Rule.Section 15: This section requires suits to be filed in the lowest grade of court having competency.
Rule.Section 16: This provision mandates that suits for recovery of immovable property be filed where the property is located.
Rule.Section 17: This section allows for suits involving property spanning multiple jurisdictions to be filed in any relevant court.
Rule.Section 18: This provision addresses procedural steps when the exact jurisdiction of immovable property is uncertain.
Rule.Section 20: This general rule places suits at the defendant's residence or where the cause of action arises.

Application.Section 15: While it dictates the court's grade, it does not specify the geographical location for property suits.
Application.Section 16: For property exclusively in Dhaka, the suit must be filed in the court where that property is situate.
Application.Section 17: This section is irrelevant here because the property is not stated to span across different court limits.
Application.Section 18: There is no allegation of uncertainty in the prompt to trigger the special rules of Section 18.
Application.Section 20: The residence of the defendant is not the primary venue for immovable property recovery under the Code.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, section 16, Clause (a), suits for the recovery of immovable property must be instituted in the court within whose local jurisdiction the property is situated. This follows the principle of 'situs' for real property. Therefore, (D) The Court within whose local limits the property is situate is correct.

Question:
The Court may give interest on costs at any rate not exceeding ________ per cent per annum.
(A) Six
(B) Four
(C) Twelve
(D) Nine

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 34
Possible Sections.Full Text: Where and in so far as a decree is for the payment of money, the Court may, in the decree, order interest at such rate as the Court deems reasonable...

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 35
Possible Sections.Full Text: The Court may give interest on costs at any rate not exceeding six per cent. per annum...

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 35A
Possible Sections.Full Text: If in any suit... any party objects to the claim... as false or vexatious... cost... may extend upto fifty thousand taka.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 35B
Possible Sections.Full Text: If at any stage of a suit... an application... is not filed within the time fixed... cost... not exceeding two thousand taka.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 13
Possible Sections.Full Text: A foreign judgment shall be conclusive... except- (f) where it sustains a claim founded on a breach of any law...

Relevant Section: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 35
Section Text: The Court may give interest on costs at any rate not exceeding six per cent. per annum, and such interest shall be added to the costs and shall be recoverable as such.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Identifying the statutory maximum interest rate applicable specifically to awarded costs.

Rule.Section 34: This section allows the court to grant reasonable interest on the principal sum of a money decree.
Rule.Section 35: This provision grants the court discretion over suit costs and limits interest on costs to six percent.
Rule.Section 35A: This section focuses on the compensatory amount for vexatious litigation rather than interest on standard costs.
Rule.Section 35B: This provision sets a flat maximum fee for procedural delays rather than a percentage-based interest rate.
Rule.Section 13: This section lists exceptions to the conclusiveness of foreign judgments and is irrelevant to domestic cost interest.

Application.Section 34: Interest on the principal sum is at the court's discretion, unlike the fixed cap for interest on costs.
Application.Section 35: Subsection (3) explicitly restricts interest on costs to a maximum of six percent per annum.
Application.Section 35A: Compensation for false claims is a separate category from the accrual of interest on legal costs.
Application.Section 35B: Delay costs are specific penalties and do not involve the calculation of annual interest percentages.
Application.Section 13: Rules on foreign judgments do not prescribe specific interest rates for civil costs in Bangladesh.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, section 35, Subsection (3), the court is empowered to award interest on legal costs. However, this interest is statutory capped at a rate not exceeding six per cent per annum. Therefore, (A) Six is correct.

Question:
Which property is expressly EXEMPT from attachment and sale in execution of a decree under Section 60 of the CPC?
(A) Shares in a corporation
(B) Necessary wearing-apparel of the judgment-debtor
(C) Government securities
(D) Cheques and bills of exchange

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 60
Possible Sections.Full Text: The following property is liable to attachment and sale in execution... Provided that... necessary wearing-apparel... shall not be liable...

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 61
Possible Sections.Full Text: The Government may... declare that such portion of agricultural produce... shall... be exempted from liability to attachment...

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 62
Possible Sections.Full Text: No person executing any process... directing... seizure of movable property shall enter any dwelling-house after sunset and before sunrise.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 63
Possible Sections.Full Text: Where property not in the custody of any Court is under attachment... the Court which shall receive... shall be the Court of highest grade...

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 64
Possible Sections.Full Text: Where an attachment has been made, any private transfer... of the property attached... shall be void...

Relevant Section: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 60
Section Text: Provided that the following particulars shall not be liable to such attachment or sale, namely:- (a) the necessary wearing-apparel, cooking vessels, beds and bedding of the judgment-debtor, his wife and children...

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Identifying which item is protected from attachment under the statutory exemptions list.

Rule.Section 60: This section lists property liable for attachment and specifically exempts basic necessities like wearing apparel.
Rule.Section 61: This provision allows the government to partially exempt agricultural produce for the survival of the debtor.
Rule.Section 62: This section regulates the timing and method of entry into dwelling-houses for seizing movable property.
Rule.Section 63: This provision determines which court has priority when property is attached by multiple courts simultaneously.
Rule.Section 64: This section declares private transfers of property void once it has been legally attached by a court.

Application.Section 60: Clause (a) of the proviso explicitly excludes necessary wearing-apparel from being sold to satisfy a decree.
Application.Section 61: Agricultural exemptions are discretionary by government notification and do not automatically cover personal apparel.
Application.Section 62: Rules on entry times for seizure do not define which specific properties are immune from attachment.
Application.Section 63: Court hierarchy rules for realization do not address the intrinsic exempt status of the judgment-debtor's clothes.
Application.Section 64: While protecting the attachment's validity, this section does not list property items that are immune from seizure.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, section 60, Subsection (1), Clause (a), necessary wearing-apparel, cooking vessels, and bedding of the judgment-debtor and their family are expressly exempt from attachment and sale. Therefore, (B) Necessary wearing-apparel of the judgment-debtor is correct.

Question:
What is the maximum amount of compensatory costs that can be awarded for false or vexatious claims under Section 35A of The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908?
(A) Fifty thousand Taka
(B) One lakh Taka
(C) Twenty thousand Taka
(D) Ten thousand Taka

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 35
Possible Sections.Full Text: Subject to such conditions and limitations as may be prescribed... the costs of and incident to all suits shall be in the discretion of the Court...

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 35A
Possible Sections.Full Text: If in any suit... any party objects... on the ground that the claim... is false or vexatious... cost... may extend upto fifty thousand taka.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 35B
Possible Sections.Full Text: If at any stage of a suit... an application... is not filed within the time fixed... cost... not exceeding two thousand taka.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 32
Possible Sections.Full Text: The Court may compel the attendance of any person... impose a fine upon him not exceeding five hundred Taka.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 95
Possible Sections.Full Text: award against the plaintiff... amount, not exceeding ten thousand Taka, as it deems a reasonable compensation to the defendant...

Relevant Section: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 35A
Section Text: the Court shall... make an order for the payment to the objector, such cost by way of compensation which may, without exceeding the limit of the Court's pecuniary jurisdiction, extend upto fifty thousand taka.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Identifying the numerical limit for compensatory costs in cases of vexatious litigation.

Rule.Section 35: This section establishes the court's general discretion to award costs incident to civil suits.
Rule.Section 35A: This provision specifically addresses false or vexatious claims, capping compensation at fifty thousand Taka.
Rule.Section 35B: This section penalizes procedural delays with small costs capped at two or three thousand Taka.
Rule.Section 32: This provision limits the fine for a witness's non-attendance to five hundred Taka.
Rule.Section 95: This section allows for up to ten thousand Taka in compensation for wrongful interlocutory orders.

Application.Section 35: General discretion over costs does not specify the fixed ceiling for punitive or compensatory vexatious costs.
Application.Section 35A: Subsection (1) explicitly provides the cap of fifty thousand Taka for false or vexatious claims.
Application.Section 35B: The two thousand Taka limit applies to filing delays, not the substantive bad faith of a claim.
Application.Section 32: Fines for witnesses are distinct from compensatory costs paid to a party for defending a false suit.
Application.Section 95: Compensation for wrongful arrest or attachment is restricted to ten thousand Taka, which is lower than the vexatious cost cap.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, section 35A, Subsection (1), if a claim or defense is found to be false or vexatious, the court may award compensatory costs. These costs are capped at fifty thousand Taka. Therefore, (A) Fifty thousand Taka is correct.

Question:
Under Section 58 of the CPC, if a decree is for a sum of money exceeding fifty Taka, the judgment-debtor may be detained for a period of —
(A) Thirty days
(B) Six months
(C) One year
(D) Six weeks

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 55
Possible Sections.Full Text: A judgment-debtor may be arrested in execution of a decree... and his detention may be in the civil prison...

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 58
Possible Sections.Full Text: Every person detained... shall be so detained... where the decree is for... money exceeding fifty Taka, for a period of six months...

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 59
Possible Sections.Full Text: At any time after a warrant... the Court may cancel it on the ground of his serious illness.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 74
Possible Sections.Full Text: order the judgment-debtor... to be detained in the civil prison for a term which may extend to thirty days...

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 32
Possible Sections.Full Text: order him to furnish security... and in default commit him to the civil prison.

Relevant Section: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 58
Section Text: Every person detained in the civil prison in execution of a decree shall be so detained,- (a) where the decree is for the payment of a sum of money exceeding fifty Taka, for a period of six months...

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Identifying the statutory maximum detention period for debt execution exceeding fifty Taka.

Rule.Section 55: This section outlines the general procedure for arresting and bringing a judgment-debtor before the court.
Rule.Section 58: This provision sets the fixed time limits for detention based on the monetary value of the decree.
Rule.Section 59: This section allows for the release of a judgment-debtor specifically on the grounds of serious illness.
Rule.Section 74: This provision limits detention for resisting the execution of a decree for property to thirty days.
Rule.Section 32: This section allows for imprisonment in default of security for witnesses but does not set the debt-based limits.

Application.Section 55: The power to arrest does not specify the duration of the subsequent detention in prison.
Application.Section 58: Subsection (1), Clause (a) explicitly mandates a six-month detention for sums exceeding fifty Taka.
Application.Section 59: Provisions for health-based release do not define the standard statutory period of detention.
Application.Section 74: Detention for resistance (30 days) is much shorter than the standard period for non-payment of a substantial debt.
Application.Section 32: Committal for witness default is a procedural enforcement measure, not a debt-recovery mechanism.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, section 58, Subsection (1), Clause (a), a judgment-debtor can be detained in civil prison for six months if the decree exceeds fifty Taka. For lesser amounts, the period is reduced. Therefore, (B) Six months is correct.

Question:
Under Section 26 of the CPC, every suit shall be instituted by the presentation of a —
(A) Written statement
(B) Application for summons
(C) Plaint
(D) Affidavit only

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 26
Possible Sections.Full Text: Every suit shall be instituted by the presentation of a plaint or in such other manner as may be prescribed.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 27
Possible Sections.Full Text: Where a suit has been duly instituted, a summons may be issued to the defendant to appear and answer...

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 30
Possible Sections.Full Text: the Court may... order any fact to be proved by affidavit.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 15
Possible Sections.Full Text: Every suit shall be instituted in the Court of the lowest grade competent to try it.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 2(2)
Possible Sections.Full Text: It shall be deemed to include the rejection of a plaint...

Relevant Section: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 26
Section Text: Every suit shall be instituted by the presentation of a plaint or in such other manner as may be prescribed.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Identifying the formal document required to commence a civil suit.

Rule.Section 26: This section mandates that all suits begin with the presentation of a document called a 'plaint'.
Rule.Section 27: This provision describes the step following institution, which is the issuance of a summons to the defendant.
Rule.Section 30: This section allows the court to require certain facts to be proven by affidavit during proceedings.
Rule.Section 15: This rule specifies the rank of the court where a suit must be filed but not the filing document.
Rule.Section 2(2): This section mentions 'plaint' in the context of its rejection being a decree, assuming its prior existence.

Application.Section 26: Subsection (1) explicitly names the 'plaint' as the instrument used to institute a suit.
Application.Section 27: Summons is a secondary step that occurs only after the suit has been 'duly instituted' by a plaint.
Application.Section 30: While affidavits are used to prove facts, they are not the primary vehicle for initiating the litigation.
Application.Section 15: Filing in the lowest grade court is a jurisdictional requirement, not a procedural document requirement.
Application.Section 2(2): Defining the effect of rejecting a plaint confirms that the plaint is the foundational document of the suit.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, section 26, Subsection (1), the mandatory procedure for initiating a civil suit is the presentation of a plaint. This document sets out the plaintiff's claim. Therefore, (C) Plaint is correct.

Question:
Under Section 34 of the CPC, if a decree for payment of money is silent regarding further interest from the date of decree to payment, the Court is deemed to have —
(A) Refused such interest
(B) Granted interest at the bank rate
(C) Granted interest at 6%
(D) Reserved the decision for a separate suit

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 33
Possible Sections.Full Text: The Court, after the case has been heard, shall pronounce judgment, and on such judgment a decree shall follow.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 34
Possible Sections.Full Text: Where such a decree is silent... the Court shall be deemed to have refused such interest...

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 35
Possible Sections.Full Text: The Court may give interest on costs at any rate not exceeding six per cent. per annum...

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 11
Possible Sections.Full Text: Any relief claimed in the plaint, which is not expressly granted by the decree... shall... be deemed to have been refused.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 2(2)
Possible Sections.Full Text: "decree" means the formal expression of an adjudication... conclusively determines the rights...

Relevant Section: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 34
Section Text: Where such a decree is silent with respect to the payment of further interest... the Court shall be deemed to have refused such interest, and a separate suit therefor shall not lie.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Identifying the legal presumption when a money decree excludes mention of post-decree interest.

Rule.Section 33: This section establishes that a decree is the formal outcome following a court's judgment.
Rule.Section 34: This provision governs the award of interest and explicitly handles the court's silence on further interest.
Rule.Section 35: This section limits interest on costs but does not govern interest on the principal decree amount.
Rule.Section 11: Explanation V of this section creates a similar 'deemed refusal' rule for res judicata purposes.
Rule.Section 2(2): This subsection defines a decree but does not address specific interest presumptions.

Application.Section 33: The sequence of judgment and decree does not inform the interpretation of silent provisions within the decree.
Application.Section 34: Subsection (2) clearly states that silence regarding further interest constitutes a legal refusal of that interest.
Application.Section 35: Cost-related interest is distinct from the interest on the principal sum adjudged in a money decree.
Application.Section 11: While logically consistent with Section 11, Section 34 is the specific provision for money decree interest.
Application.Section 2(2): The definition of a decree focuses on finality rather than specific defaults in interest calculations.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, section 34, Subsection (2), if a money decree is silent on further interest from the date of the decree until payment, it is legally deemed that the court has refused such interest. Therefore, (A) Refused such interest is correct.

Type: T08 — Procedural Consequence / What Happens Next

Question:
A decree may be executed by the Court which passed it, or by the —
(A) Court to which it is sent for execution
(B) High Court Division only
(C) District Court of the capital
(D) Court of Small Causes only

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 36
Possible Sections.Full Text: The provisions of this Code relating to the execution of decrees shall... be deemed to apply to the execution of orders.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 37
Possible Sections.Full Text: The expression "Court which passed a decree," or words to that effect, shall... be deemed to include...

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 38
Possible Sections.Full Text: A decree may be executed either by the Court which passed it, or by the Court to which it is sent for execution.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 39
Possible Sections.Full Text: The Court which passed a decree may, on the application of the decree-holder, send it for execution to another Court...

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 41
Possible Sections.Full Text: The Court to which a decree is sent for execution shall certify to the Court which passed it the fact of such execution...

Relevant Section: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 38
Section Text: A decree may be executed either by the Court which passed it, or by the Court to which it is sent for execution.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Identifying which courts have the authority to carry out the execution of a decree.

Rule.Section 36: This section makes the rules for executing decrees applicable to court orders as well.
Rule.Section 37: This provision defines what qualifies as the 'Court which passed a decree' for execution purposes.
Rule.Section 38: This section specifies the two types of courts that can execute a decree: the original court or the transferee court.
Rule.Section 39: This provision describes the conditions under which a court can transfer its decree to another court for execution.
Rule.Section 41: This section requires the transferee court to report the result of execution back to the original court.

Application.Section 36: General application to orders does not answer which specific court handles the execution process.
Application.Section 37: Defining the 'passing court' is preparatory and does not list the execution venues themselves.
Application.Section 38: The text directly states that a decree is executed by the passing court or the court it was sent to.
Application.Section 39: While authorizing the 'sending' of a decree, Section 38 is the primary rule stating 'who' executes.
Application.Section 41: Reporting duties follow the execution process and do not define the executing court's identity initially.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, section 38, the law provides that a decree can be executed by two potential venues: the Court that originally passed the decree or the specific Court to which it has been sent for execution. Therefore, (A) Court to which it is sent for execution is correct.

Question:
Under Section 89A of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, which of the following is correct regarding the nature of mediation proceedings?
(A) The mediator can be called as a witness to testify about admissions made
(B) A report of the mediation must be published in the official Gazette
(C) They are open to the public
(D) They are confidential and inadmissible in evidence in subsequent hearings

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 88
Possible Sections.Full Text: Where two or more persons claim adversely to one another the same debt... such other person may institute a suit of interpleader...

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 89A
Possible Sections.Full Text: The proceedings of mediation under this section shall be confidential and any communication made... shall be deemed privileged and shall not be referred to and admissible in evidence in any subsequent hearing...

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 89B
Possible Sections.Full Text: If the parties to a suit... apply to the Court for withdrawal of the suit on ground that they will refer the dispute... to arbitration... the Court shall allow the application...

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 90
Possible Sections.Full Text: Where any persons agree in writing to state a case for the opinion of the Court, then the Court shall try and determine the same in the manner prescribed.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 122
Possible Sections.Full Text: The Supreme Court may, from time to time after previous publication, make rules regulating the procedure of each Division of the Supreme Court...

Relevant Section: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 89A
Section Text: The proceedings of mediation under this section shall be confidential and any communication made, evidence adduced, admission, statement or comment made and conversation held between the parties, their pleaders, representatives [, Legal Aid Officer] and the mediator, shall be deemed privileged and shall not be referred to and admissible in evidence in any subsequent hearing of the same suit or any other proceeding.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Identifying the statutory confidentiality and privilege rules governing court-mandated mediation.

Rule.Section 88: This section governs the institution of interpleader suits by neutral stakeholders.
Rule.Section 89A: This provision mandates that mediation proceedings are confidential, privileged, and inadmissible in future evidence.
Rule.Section 89B: This section allows parties to withdraw a suit to pursue settlement via arbitration.
Rule.Section 90: This provision describes the procedure for stating a special case for the court's opinion.
Rule.Section 122: This section empowers the Supreme Court to frame rules regulating judicial procedures.

Application.Section 88: Interpleader rules focus on adverse claims rather than the confidentiality of settlement discussions.
Application.Section 89A: Subsection (8) explicitly protects all mediation communications from being used as evidence in subsequent hearings.
Application.Section 89B: Arbitration withdrawal is a distinct ADR mechanism and does not define mediation's privacy standards.
Application.Section 90: Special cases involve formal adjudication of agreed facts, which is contrary to confidential settlement mediation.
Application.Section 122: Rule-making power is a broad administrative function and not a specific rule on mediation privilege.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, section 89A, Subsection (8), mediation proceedings are strictly confidential. Any communication or admission made during these proceedings is privileged and cannot be admitted as evidence in subsequent hearings. Therefore, (D) They are confidential and inadmissible in evidence in subsequent hearings is correct.

Question:
If parties to a suit apply to refer their dispute to arbitration under Section 89B of the CPC, the Court shall —
(A) Stay the suit until an award is passed
(B) Dismiss the suit with costs
(C) Allow the application and permit the suit to be withdrawn
(D) Refer the matter to a mediator first

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 89A
Possible Sections.Full Text: after filing of written statement... [the Court shall]... mediate in order to settle the dispute... or refer the dispute... to a mediator...

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 89B
Possible Sections.Full Text: If the parties... apply to the Court for withdrawal of the suit on ground that they will refer the dispute... to arbitration... the Court shall allow the application and permit the suit to be withdrawn...

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 10
Possible Sections.Full Text: No Court shall proceed with the trial of any suit in which the matter in issue is also directly and substantially in issue in a previously instituted suit...

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 12
Possible Sections.Full Text: Where a plaintiff is precluded by rules from instituting a further suit... he shall not be entitled to institute a suit...

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 22
Possible Sections.Full Text: Where a suit may be instituted in any one of two or more Courts... any defendant... may... apply to have the suit transferred...

Relevant Section: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 89B
Section Text: If the parties to a suit, at any stage of the proceeding, apply to the Court for withdrawal of the suit on ground that they will refer the dispute or disputes in the suit to arbitration for settlement, the Court shall allow the application and permit the suit to be withdrawn; and the dispute or disputes, thereafter, shall be settled in accordance with Salish Ain, 2001 (Act No. 1 of 2001) so far as may be applicable.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Determining the court's required response to an application for arbitration-based withdrawal.

Rule.Section 89A: This provision describes the court's duty to initiate or refer a suit for mediation.
Rule.Section 89B: This section mandates the court to allow suit withdrawal if parties choose to settle via arbitration.
Rule.Section 10: This section governs the stay of suits involving identical issues pending in other courts.
Rule.Section 12: This provision bars new suits when existing rules prohibit further litigation on the same cause.
Rule.Section 22: This section allows for the transfer of a suit that could have been filed in multiple venues.

Application.Section 89A: Mediation usually involves an adjournment or referral, whereas arbitration requires a formal withdrawal.
Application.Section 89B: The statute explicitly directs the court to allow the withdrawal application so parties can use the Salish Ain, 2001.
Application.Section 10: Staying a suit for 'res sub judice' is a mandatory trial bar, not a voluntary alternative dispute resolution exit.
Application.Section 12: Bars on future suits don't apply here as Section 89B specifically allows re-institution if arbitration fails.
Application.Section 22: Transferring a suit keeps it within the court system, unlike arbitration which moves it to an out-of-court tribunal.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, section 89B, Subsection (1), when parties apply to refer their dispute to arbitration, the court is required to allow the application and permit the suit to be withdrawn. Therefore, (C) Allow the application and permit the suit to be withdrawn is correct.

Question:
Who may institute a suit for a declaration and injunction in the case of a public nuisance under Section 91 of the CPC?
(A) The Secretary of the local municipality
(B) The District Magistrate only
(C) The Attorney General, or two or more persons with his written consent
(D) Any person residing in the locality

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 79
Possible Sections.Full Text: In a suit by or against the Government the authority to be named as plaintiff or defendant... shall be Bangladesh.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 91
Possible Sections.Full Text: In the case of a public nuisance the Attorney General, or two or more persons having obtained the consent in writing of the Attorney General, may institute a suit...

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 92
Possible Sections.Full Text: In the case of any alleged breach of any express or constructive trust... the Attorney General, or two or more persons... having obtained the consent in writing of the Attorney General...

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 93
Possible Sections.Full Text: The powers conferred by sections 91 and 92 on the Attorney General may... be... exercised also by the Collector...

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 2(7)
Possible Sections.Full Text: "Government Pleader" includes any officer appointed by the Government to perform all or any of the functions expressly imposed by this Code...

Relevant Section: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 91
Section Text: In the case of a public nuisance the Attorney General, or two or more persons having obtained the consent in writing of the Attorney General, may institute a suit, though no special damage has been caused, for a declaration and injunction or for such other relief as may be appropriate to the circumstances of the case.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Identifying the authorized parties for initiating legal action against a public nuisance.

Rule.Section 79: This section mandates that suits against the government must be filed in the name of Bangladesh.
Rule.Section 91: This provision authorizes the Attorney General or two persons with his consent to sue for public nuisance.
Rule.Section 92: This section governs suits related to public charities, using a similar consent mechanism from the Attorney General.
Rule.Section 93: This provision allows the Collector to exercise the Attorney General's powers under Sections 91 and 92 if sanctioned.
Rule.Section 2(7): This subsection defines a Government Pleader as an officer appointed to perform specific legal functions for the state.

Application.Section 79: General rules for naming the state do not define who has the standing to sue for community-wide wrongs.
Application.Section 91: Subsection (1) explicitly names the Attorney General and consenting private pairs as the proper plaintiffs.
Application.Section 92: While the standing is similar, Section 92 applies specifically to charitable trusts rather than public nuisances.
Application.Section 93: The Collector can only act if the Government grants specific sanction, making option B the primary rule.
Application.Section 2(7): A Government Pleader assists in suits but does not replace the specific standing required for Section 91 actions.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, section 91, Subsection (1), a suit for public nuisance can be instituted by the Attorney General. Alternatively, two or more persons can sue if they obtain the Attorney General's written consent. Therefore, (C) The Attorney General, or two or more persons with his written consent is correct.

Question:
What is the maximum compensation a defendant can be awarded for an arrest or attachment on insufficient grounds under Section 95 of The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908?
(A) Fifty thousand Taka
(B) Ten thousand Taka
(C) Five thousand Taka
(D) One lakh Taka

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 95
Possible Sections.Full Text: Where... an arrest or attachment has been effected... on insufficient grounds... award against the plaintiff... amount, not exceeding ten thousand Taka...

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 35A
Possible Sections.Full Text: If in any suit... claim or defence... is false or vexatious... cost... extend upto fifty thousand taka.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 35B
Possible Sections.Full Text: If at any stage... an application... is not filed within the time fixed... cost... not exceeding two thousand taka.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 32
Possible Sections.Full Text: The Court may compel the attendance of any person... impose a fine upon him not exceeding five hundred Taka.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 58
Possible Sections.Full Text: where the decree is for the payment of a sum of money exceeding fifty Taka, for a period of six months...

Relevant Section: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 95
Section Text: award against the plaintiff by its order such amount, not exceeding ten thousand Taka, as it deems a reasonable compensation to the defendant for the expense or injury caused to him: Provided that a Court shall not award, under this section, an amount exceeding the limits of its pecuniary jurisdiction.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Identifying the statutory limit for compensation awarded for wrongful interlocutory orders.

Rule.Section 95: This section limits compensation for wrongful arrest, attachment, or injunction to ten thousand Taka.
Rule.Section 35A: This provision caps compensatory costs for false or vexatious claims at fifty thousand Taka.
Rule.Section 35B: This section limits costs for filing delays to a maximum of two thousand Taka.
Rule.Section 32: This provision restricts fines for a witness's non-attendance to five hundred Taka.
Rule.Section 58: This section outlines the duration of civil detention for debts exceeding fifty Taka.

Application.Section 95: Subsection (1) explicitly states the court may award an amount 'not exceeding ten thousand Taka' for the injury caused.
Application.Section 35A: While both involve compensation, Section 35A addresses the merits of the case rather than wrongful interim orders.
Application.Section 35B: Costs for procedural delays are not intended as compensation for the harm of wrongful arrest.
Application.Section 32: Fines levied against witnesses go to the state, whereas Section 95 compensation goes to the aggrieved defendant.
Application.Section 58: Detention duration is a timeframe for imprisonment and not a monetary award for damages.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, section 95, Subsection (1), a defendant who has been wrongly arrested or attached can seek compensation. The court is authorized to award an amount not exceeding ten thousand Taka for the resulting injury. Therefore, (B) Ten thousand Taka is correct.

Question:
Under Section 96 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, an appeal may lie from which of the following?
(A) A decree passed with the consent of parties
(B) A judgment of a small causes court on facts
(C) An original decree passed ex parte
(D) An order of the High Court Division in its original jurisdiction

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 96
Possible Sections.Full Text: (1) ...an appeal shall lie from every decree passed... (2) An appeal may lie from an original decree passed ex parte. (3) No appeal shall lie from a decree passed... with the consent of parties.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 97
Possible Sections.Full Text: Where any party aggrieved by a preliminary decree... does not appeal... he shall be precluded from disputing its correctness... in any appeal... from the final decree.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 98
Possible Sections.Full Text: Where an appeal is heard by a Bench of two or more Judges, the appeal shall be decided in accordance with the opinion... of the majority...

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 99
Possible Sections.Full Text: No decree shall be reserved or substantially varied... on account of any misjoinder of parties... not affecting the merits...

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 104
Possible Sections.Full Text: An appeal shall lie from the following orders... (ff) an order under section 35A; (g) an order under section 95...

Relevant Section: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 96
Section Text: An appeal may lie from an original decree passed ex parte.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Identifying the specific types of decrees that are appealable under original jurisdiction.

Rule.Section 96: This section generally allows appeals from decrees, including ex parte ones, but bars consent decrees.
Rule.Section 97: This provision bars challenging a preliminary decree if an appeal was not filed before the final decree.
Rule.Section 98: This section dictates the decision-making process for appellate benches of two or more judges.
Rule.Section 99: This provision prevents the reversal of a decree for technical errors that do not affect the case's merits.
Rule.Section 104: This section lists specific non-decree orders from which an appeal is permitted.

Application.Section 96: Subsection (2) explicitly states that an appeal lies from an ex parte decree, while subsection (3) explicitly bars appeals from consent decrees.
Application.Section 97: Rules on preliminary decrees do not apply to the general right to appeal an ex parte adjudication.
Application.Section 98: Administrative rules for judges do not determine whether a specific type of decree can be appealed.
Application.Section 99: Merit-based error rules govern how an appeal is decided, not whether the appeal is maintainable initially.
Application.Section 104: This section deals with orders, not original decrees, which are governed by Section 96.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, section 96, Subsection (2), the law specifically allows an appeal from an original decree passed ex parte. Conversely, Subsection (3) prohibits appeals from decrees passed with the parties' consent. Therefore, (C) An original decree passed ex parte is correct.

Question:
Under Section 99 of the CPC, no decree shall be reversed or modified for an error that —
(A) Affects the merits of the case
(B) Does NOT affect the merits of the case or the jurisdiction of the Court
(C) Affects the jurisdiction of the Court
(D) Relates to the payment of court fees

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 96
Possible Sections.Full Text: an appeal shall lie from every decree passed by any Court exercising original jurisdiction...

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 99
Possible Sections.Full Text: No decree shall be reserved... in appeal on account of any misjoinder... or any error... not affecting the merits of the case or the jurisdiction...

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 107
Possible Sections.Full Text: an Appellate Court shall have power- (a) to determine a case finally; (b) to remand a case...

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 151
Possible Sections.Full Text: N/A — No single provision directly governs.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 152
Possible Sections.Full Text: N/A — No single provision directly governs.

Relevant Section: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 99
Section Text: No decree shall be reserved or substantially varied, nor shall any case be remanded, in appeal on account of any misjoinder of parties or causes of action or any error, defect or irregularity in any proceedings in the suit, not affecting the merits of the case or the jurisdiction of the Court.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Identifying the statutory rule against reversing decrees for non-prejudicial technical errors.

Rule.Section 96: This section provides the general right to appeal against a court's original decree.
Rule.Section 99: This provision bars the reversal of decrees for technical irregularities that don't impact merits or jurisdiction.
Rule.Section 107: This section outlines the general powers of an appellate court, including the power to remand cases.
Rule.Section 151: This section relates to the inherent powers of the court to meet the ends of justice.
Rule.Section 152: This provision allows for the correction of clerical or arithmetical mistakes in judgments or decrees.

Application.Section 96: While granting the right to appeal, this section does not set the standard for when a decree should be overturned.
Application.Section 99: The statute explicitly protects decrees from being reversed for errors that 'do not affect the merits or jurisdiction'.
Application.Section 107: General appellate powers are subject to the specific limitation against trivial remands or reversals found in Section 99.
Application.Section 151: Inherent powers are used to provide remedies, not to define the criteria for upholding decrees on appeal.
Application.Section 152: Correcting a typo is a clerical act, which is different from reversing a decree due to procedural defects.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, section 99, a decree cannot be reversed or substantially varied in an appeal based on technical errors or irregularities. This protection applies as long as the error does not affect the merits of the case or the court's jurisdiction. Therefore, (B) Does NOT affect the merits of the case or the jurisdiction of the Court is correct.

Question:
Under Section 104 of the CPC, an appeal lies from which of the following orders?

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Identifying specific interlocutory orders that are subject to an independent right of appeal.

Rule.Section 96: This section governs appeals from original decrees but does not cover interlocutory orders.
Rule.Section 104: This provision lists specific orders, such as those under Section 35A or 95, from which an appeal lies.
Rule.Section 105: This section generally bars appeals from orders unless specifically provided, allowing them to be challenged only via the final decree.
Rule.Section 114: This provision provides the remedy of review for orders or decrees where no appeal was filed.
Rule.Section 2(2): This subsection clarifies that orders appealable under Section 104 are explicitly excluded from the definition of a decree.

Application.Section 96: Original decrees are final adjudications, whereas Section 35A orders are specific cost-related decisions.
Application.Section 104: Clause (ff) of subsection (1) explicitly permits an appeal against an order made under Section 35A.
Application.Section 105: Since Section 104 'expressly provides' for Section 35A orders, they are exempt from the Section 105 bar.
Application.Section 114: The right to review is an alternative that assumes the person has the initial right to appeal mentioned in Section 104.
Application.Section 2(2): An order under Section 35A is a classic example of an adjudication that is appealable as an 'appeal from an order'.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, section 104, Subsection (1), Clause (ff), an appeal is expressly allowed from an order passed under Section 35A concerning compensatory costs. This is one of the few interlocutory orders with a separate right of appeal. Therefore, (D) An order under Section 35A regarding compensatory costs is correct.

Question:

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 99
Possible Sections.Full Text: No decree shall be reserved... nor shall any case be remanded, in appeal on account of any misjoinder...

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Identifying the statutory powers granted to a court while exercising its appellate jurisdiction.

Rule.Section 99: This provision limits the appellate power to remand if the error was purely technical and non-merit based.
Rule.Section 107: This section provides four primary powers to appellate courts: final determination, remand, framing issues, and taking evidence.
Rule.Section 108: This section extends the procedural rules of original appeals to appeals from appellate decrees and orders.
Rule.Section 115: This provision defines the revisional powers of the High Court and District Court over non-appealable cases.
Rule.Section 151: This section reserves the court's inherent power to act for justice when no specific provision applies.

Application.Section 99: While Section 99 restricts the 'use' of remand, it does not define the 'power' to remand itself.
Application.Section 107: Subsection (1), Clause (b) explicitly lists 'to remand a case' as one of the four essential appellate powers.
Application.Section 108: Procedure extension does not add new powers beyond those already defined in Section 107.
Application.Section 115: Revision is a distinct jurisdiction from appeal and involves calling for records rather than remanding for trial.
Application.Section 151: Inherent powers are secondary to the express statutory powers enumerated in Section 107.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, section 107, Subsection (1), Clause (b), an Appellate Court is expressly granted the power to remand a case back to the lower court. This is part of its broad mandate to ensure proper adjudication. Therefore, (C) Remand a case is correct.

Question:
Under Section 113 of the CPC, any Court may state a case and refer it for the opinion of which authority?
(A) The Supreme Court, Appellate Division
(B) The Attorney General
(C) The High Court Division
(D) The District Judge

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 113
Possible Sections.Full Text: any Court may state a case and refer the same for the opinion of the High Court Division...

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 114
Possible Sections.Full Text: any person considering himself aggrieved... by a decision on a reference from a Court of Small Causes may apply for a view...

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 115
Possible Sections.Full Text: The High Court Division may... call for the record of any suit or proceedings... from which no appeal lies...

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 111
Possible Sections.Full Text: Notwithstanding anything contained in section 109, no appeal shall lie to the [Appellate Division]— (a) from the decree or order of one Judge...

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 90
Possible Sections.Full Text: Where any persons agree in writing to state a case for the opinion of the Court, then the Court shall try and determine the same...

Relevant Section: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 113
Section Text: Subject to such conditions and limitations as may be prescribed, any Court may state a case and refer the same for the opinion of the High Court Division, and the High Court Division may make such order thereon as it thinks fit.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Identifying the designated court for receiving and deciding on a legal reference.

Rule.Section 113: This section allows any civil court to refer a case for the High Court Division's opinion.
Rule.Section 114: This provision describes the review process, including when it follows a reference decision from Small Causes.
Rule.Section 115: This section establishes the revisional jurisdiction of the High Court over non-appealable lower court orders.
Rule.Section 111: This provision bars certain direct appeals to the Appellate Division from single-judge High Court decisions.
Rule.Section 90: This section governs 'special cases' where parties voluntarily agree to seek a court's opinion on a stated matter.

Application.Section 113: The statute explicitly mandates that the reference must be made to the High Court Division for its legal opinion.
Application.Section 114: Review happens 'after' a decision is made, whereas Section 113 is a request for a decision during the process.
Application.Section 115: Revision involves correcting legal errors in existing orders, while reference is a proactive request for legal guidance.
Application.Section 111: Appellate Division bars are irrelevant to the initial referral of a case for a legal opinion from a lower court.
Application.Section 90: Special cases are initiated by parties, while references under Section 113 are initiated by the court itself.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, section 113, any civil court has the authority to state a case and refer it for the legal opinion of the High Court Division. This procedure is used to clarify uncertain points of law. Therefore, (C) The High Court Division is correct.

Question:
A person aggrieved by a decree can apply for a review under Section 114 of The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908 if —
(A) The High Court Division has already decided the case in revision

(D) The decree was passed by the Appellate Division

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 113
Possible Sections.Full Text: any Court may state a case and refer the same for the opinion of the High Court Division...

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 114
Possible Sections.Full Text: any person... aggrieved- (a) by a decree... from which an appeal is allowed... but from which no appeal has been preferred... may apply for a view...

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 115
Possible Sections.Full Text: The High Court Division may... revise such decree or order... from which no appeal lies...

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 96
Possible Sections.Full Text: an appeal shall lie from every decree passed... No appeal shall lie from a decree passed... with the consent of parties.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 21
Possible Sections.Full Text: No objection as to the place of suing shall be allowed by any appellate or revisional Court unless such objection was taken...

Relevant Section: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 114
Section Text: Subject as aforesaid, any person considering himself aggrieved- (a) by a decree or order from which an appeal is allowed by this Code, but from which no appeal has been preferred, (b) by a decree or order from which no appeal is allowed by this Code, or (c) by a decision on a reference from a Court of Small Causes may apply for a view of judgment to the Court which passed the decree or made the order, and the Court may make such order thereon as it thinks fit.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Identifying the specific condition that permits a party to seek a review of a judgment.

Rule.Section 113: This section allows lower courts to refer legal questions to the High Court Division.
Rule.Section 114: This provision lists three conditions for review, including cases where an appeal is possible but wasn't filed.
Rule.Section 115: This section defines revisional power for cases where no appeal lies to the higher court.
Rule.Section 96: This section establishes the general right of appeal and specific bars like consent decrees.
Rule.Section 21: This provision limits when jurisdictional objections can be raised in higher courts.

Application.Section 113: Reference is a tool for the court to get help, while review is a remedy for an aggrieved person.
Application.Section 114: Clause (a) explicitly allows for a review when an appealable decree remains unappealed by the party.
Application.Section 115: Revision is only for non-appealable cases, whereas review (Clause a) specifically covers appealable ones.
Application.Section 96: While Section 96 defines the right to appeal, Section 114 defines what happens when that right is not exercised.
Application.Section 21: Procedural bars in appeals do not govern the availability of a review in the court of first instance.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, section 114, Clause (a), a person can apply for a review of a decree if an appeal is allowed by the Code but has not actually been preferred. Question:

(B) The Rule Committee
(C) The Ministry of Law

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 121
