Possible Sections.Full Text: The Supreme Court may... make rules regulating the procedure of each Division... and the procedure of Civil Courts...

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 123
Possible Sections.Full Text: A Committee, to be called the Rule Committee, shall be constituted for the purpose referred to in section 122.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 126
Possible Sections.Full Text: Rules made under the foregoing provisions shall be subject to the previous approval of the President.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 128
Possible Sections.Full Text: Such rules shall be not inconsistent with the provisions in the body of this Code...

Relevant Section: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 122
Section Text: 122. The Supreme Court may, from time to time after previous publication, make rules regulating the procedure of each Division of the Supreme Court and the procedure of Civil Courts subject to its Superintendence and may by such rules annul, alter or add to all or any of the rules in the First Schedule.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Identifying the primary authority empowered to create and modify the rules of civil procedure.

Rule.Section 121: This section clarifies that the rules in the First Schedule are as legally binding as the Code itself.
Rule.Section 122: This provision specifically grants the Supreme Court the power to make, alter, or add to procedural rules.
Rule.Section 123: This section mandates the creation of a 'Rule Committee' to advise on and report on proposed rule changes.
Rule.Section 126: This provision requires that all rules made by the Supreme Court must receive the President's prior approval.
Rule.Section 128: This section lists the specific topics and areas that the new procedural rules may cover.

Application.Section 121: Binding status of rules does not identify the body with the active power to change them.
Application.Section 122: The text explicitly names the 'Supreme Court' as the body authorized to make rules for Civil Courts.
Application.Section 123: While the Rule Committee assists, the actual power to 'make' the rules remains with the Supreme Court.
Application.Section 126: Approval by the President is a final check, but the Supreme Court is the entity that originates and 'makes' the rules.
Application.Section 128: The list of topics for rules is secondary to identifying the authority (the Supreme Court) that creates them.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, section 122, the Supreme Court is the body empowered to make rules regulating the procedure of civil courts. These rules can annul or alter the existing rules in the First Schedule. Therefore, (A) The Supreme Court is correct.

Question:

Application.Order IX, Rule 13: The statute explicitly requires proof that either service failed or there was a 'sufficient cause' preventing the defendant's presence.
Application.Order IX, Rule 9: This rule is a mismatch as it applies to the 'plaintiff' seeking to undo a dismissal, not a 'defendant' undoing a decree.
Application.Order IX, Rule 7: Rule 7 applies only 'during' the trial phase, whereas Rule 13 is the correct remedy 'after' the decree has been passed.
Application.Section 114: Review is a broader remedy that does not focus on the procedural default of non-appearance specifically.
Application.Order V, Rule 20: Compliance with substituted service rules is used to determine if the summons was 'duly served' as required by Rule 13.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Order IX, Rule 13, a defendant can have an ex parte decree set aside by proving that the summons was not properly served or that they were prevented by sufficient cause from attending the hearing. Therefore, (C) The summons was not duly served or he was prevented by sufficient cause from appearing is correct.

Relevant Section: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908 (Schedule 1), Order XXXIX, Rule 3
Section Text: The Court shall in all cases, before granting an injunction, direct notice of the application for the same to be given to the opposite party... Provided that... the Court may, where it appears that the object of granting injunction would be defeated by the delay, dispense with such notice.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Identifying the statutory exception that allows the court to grant an ex parte temporary injunction without prior notice.

Rule.Order XXXIX, Rule 3: This rule mandates notice before an injunction but allows the court to waive it if delay would defeat the purpose.
Rule.Order XXXIX, Rule 1: This provision establishes the substantive grounds (like waste or damage) for seeking a temporary injunction.
Rule.Order XXXIX, Rule 4: This section provides the procedure for an aggrieved party to challenge or set aside an existing injunction order.
Rule.Section 80: This provision requires a mandatory pre-suit notice period specifically for actions brought against the state.
Rule.Order V, Rule 1: This rule governs the standard issuance of a summons at the beginning of a lawsuit.

Application.Order XXXIX, Rule 3: The proviso explicitly authorizes the court to 'dispense with such notice' if the urgency makes notice counter-productive.
Application.Order XXXIX, Rule 1: Factual grounds for an injunction exist independently of the procedural requirement to notify the other side first.
Application.Order XXXIX, Rule 4: Setting aside an injunction is a subsequent remedy and not the rule for initial notice dispensation.
Application.Section 80: Notice to the government is a jurisdictional requirement for filing, whereas Rule 3 notice is an interlocutory procedure.
Application.Order V, Rule 1: Trial summonses are distinct from the specific and urgent notification required for temporary restraining orders.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Order XXXIX, Rule 3, the Court is generally required to give notice to the opposite party before granting an injunction. However, the proviso allows this notice to be dispensed with if the court believes the delay would defeat the injunction's purpose. Therefore, (C) The object of granting the injunction would be defeated by the delay is correct.

Cited Acts and Sections: Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Order XXXIX, Rule 3

Question:
Under Order XL, Rule 1, a receiver appointed by the Court is an officer of the —
(A) Defendant
(B) Plaintiff
(C) Court
(D) Government

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908 (Schedule 1), Order XL, Rule 1
Possible Sections.Full Text: Where it appears to the Court to be just and convenient, the Court may by order- (a) appoint a receiver of any property...

Possible Sections.Full Text: Every receiver so appointed shall- (a) furnish such security... as the Court thinks fit... (c) submit his accounts at such periods and in such form as the Court directs...

Possible Sections.Full Text: "public officer" means a person falling under any of the following descriptions... (d) every officer of a Court of Justice whose duty it is... to take charge or dispose of any property...

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908 (Schedule 1), Order III, Rule 2
Possible Sections.Full Text: The recognized agents of parties by whom such appearances, applications and acts may be made or done are—

Relevant Section: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908 (Schedule 1), Order XL, Rule 1
Section Text: Where it appears to the Court to be just and convenient, the Court may by order- (a) appoint a receiver of any property, whether before or after decree;

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Identifying the legal status and affiliation of a court-appointed receiver.

Rule.Order XL, Rule 1: This rule empowers the court to appoint a receiver to take custody of property in dispute.
Rule.Order XL, Rule 3: This provision lists the specific duties and accountabilities of the receiver toward the court.
Rule.Order XL, Rule 4: This section provides for enforcement actions against receivers who fail in their court-mandated duties.
Rule.Section 2(17): This subsection defines public officers to include any officer of a Court of Justice tasked with property management.
Rule.Order III, Rule 2: This rule defines the private agents who act specifically for the litigating parties.

Application.Order XL, Rule 1: A receiver is appointed by the 'Court' to hold property in 'custodia legis', making them an officer of the institution itself.
Application.Order XL, Rule 3: The receiver's duty to furnish security and submit accounts to the 'Court' confirms their role as a judicial instrument.
Application.Order XL, Rule 4: Liability for property attachment by the court for management failures reflects an internal judicial disciplinary power.
Application.Section 2(17): Statutory definitions of court officers clearly encompass those authorized to take charge of assets under judicial order.
Application.Order III, Rule 2: Receivers are distinct from party agents as they represent neither side but rather the authority of the court.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Order XL, Rule 1, a receiver is a person appointed by the Court to protect and manage property in litigation. Legally, the receiver is an officer of the Court and acts for the benefit of all parties. Therefore, (C) is correct.

Question:
Under Order XLI, Rule 27, an Appellate Court may allow additional evidence to be produced if —
(A) The Appellate Court requires it to enable it to pronounce judgment
(B) The appellant simply forgot to produce it in the lower court
(C) The evidence was discovered after the suit was instituted
(D) The opposite party does not object to the evidence

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908 (Schedule 1), Order XLI, Rule 27
Possible Sections.Full Text: But if— ... (b) the Appellate Court requires any document to be produced or any witness to be examined to enable it to pronounce judgment... the Appellate Court may allow such evidence...

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908 (Schedule 1), Order XLI, Rule 23
Possible Sections.Full Text: Where the Court from whose decree an appeal is preferred has disposed of the suit upon a preliminary point... the Appellate Court may... remand the case...

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908 (Schedule 1), Order XLI, Rule 33
Possible Sections.Full Text: The Appellate Court shall have power to pass any decree and make any order which ought to have been passed or made...

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908 (Schedule 1), Order XVIII, Rule 17
Possible Sections.Full Text: The Court may at any stage of a suit recall any witness who has been examined...

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908 (Schedule 1), Section 107
Possible Sections.Full Text: Subject to such conditions and limitations as may be prescribed, an Appellate Court shall have power- ... (d) to take additional evidence or to require such evidence to be taken.

Relevant Section: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908 (Schedule 1), Order XLI, Rule 27
Section Text: (1) The parties to an appeal shall not be entitled to produce additional evidence... in the Appellate Court. But if— ... (b) the Appellate Court requires any document to be produced or any witness to be examined to enable it to pronounce judgment, or for any other substantial cause, the Appellate Court may allow such evidence... to be produced...

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Identifying the statutory condition for introducing new evidence during an appeal.

Rule.Order XLI, Rule 27: This rule generally bars new evidence in appeal but allows it if the court requires it to pronounce judgment.
Rule.Order XLI, Rule 23: This provision governs the remand of cases for re-trial rather than the direct admission of new evidence by the appellate court.
Rule.Order XLI, Rule 33: This section grants the appellate court broad power to pass any appropriate decree or order in the interest of justice.
Rule.Order XVIII, Rule 17: This rule allows a trial court to recall and re-examine witnesses during the original hearing of the suit.
Rule.Section 107: This section provides the substantive legal authority for appellate courts to exercise powers like taking additional evidence.

Application.Order XLI, Rule 27: Clause (b) explicitly permits new evidence when 'the Appellate Court requires' it to facilitate a proper judgment.
Application.Order XLI, Rule 23: Remanding a case is a different procedural path than the court deciding the matter itself using new evidence.
Application.Order XLI, Rule 33: While powerful, Rule 33 relates to the 'content' of the decree rather than the 'evidentiary basis' regulated by Rule 27.
Application.Order XVIII, Rule 17: Witness recall is a trial stage procedure and does not govern the restricted evidentiary scope of an appeal.
Application.Section 107: The general power to take evidence is limited by the specific conditions set out in the corresponding procedural rule, Order XLI Rule 27.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Order XLI, Rule 27, Subsection (1), Clause (b), an Appellate Court can allow additional evidence if it requires a document or witness to enable it to pronounce judgment. Parties are not entitled to produce new evidence as a matter of right. Therefore, (A) The Appellate Court requires it to enable it to pronounce judgment is correct.

Cited Acts and Sections: Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Order XLI, Rule 27

Question:
Under Order XLIII, Rule 1, an appeal shall lie from which of the following orders?
(A) An order returning a document to a witness
(B) An order of attachment of property under Order XVI, Rule 10
(C) An order rejecting a plaint under Order VII, Rule 11
(D) An order framing issues under Order XIV

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908 (Schedule 1), Order XLIII, Rule 1

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Identifying which interlocutory order is specifically listed as appealable under Order XLIII.

Rule.Order XLIII, Rule 1: This rule provides a comprehensive list of non-decree orders that can be independently appealed.
Rule.Order VII, Rule 12: This provision describes the procedure for rejecting a plaint, which is legally defined as a decree rather than an order.
Rule.Order XIV, Rule 1: This rule mandates the framing of issues by the court but does not provide a right of appeal for this procedural step.
Rule.Section 104: This section serves as the substantive authority for appeals against specific orders mentioned in the Code.
Rule.Order IX, Rule 13: This section provides a remedy for vacating an ex parte decree, which is challenged through a separate application.

Application.Order XLIII, Rule 1: Clause (g) explicitly identifies orders of attachment under Order XVI, Rule 10 as being appealable.
Application.Order VII, Rule 12: A plaint rejection is a decree under Section 2(2), making it appealable under Section 96, not Order XLIII.
Application.Order XIV, Rule 1: Framing issues is an intermediate procedural act that is not included in the list of appealable orders.
Application.Section 104: Section 104 refers to Order XLIII to define the specific 'appealable orders', confirming the link between the two.
Application.Order IX, Rule 13: While an order 'rejecting' a Rule 13 application is appealable under Clause (d), the initial application itself is not the subject of Order XLIII.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Order XLIII, Rule 1, Clause (g), an appeal is expressly permitted against an order issued under Order XVI, Rule 10 for the attachment of property. Question:
Under Section 10 of the CPC, a Court shall NOT proceed with the trial of any suit if —
(A) The court fee has not been paid
(B) The defendant is a non-resident
(C) The plaintiff has not filed an affidavit
(D) The matter is also in issue in a previously instituted suit between the same parties

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908 (Schedule 1), Section 10
Possible Sections.Full Text: No Court shall proceed with the trial of any suit in which the matter in issue is also directly and substantially in issue in a previously instituted suit...

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908 (Schedule 1), Section 11
Possible Sections.Full Text: No Court shall try any suit or issue in which the matter directly and substantially in issue has been... finally decided by such Court.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908 (Schedule 1), Section 12
Possible Sections.Full Text: Where a plaintiff is precluded by rules from instituting a further suit... he shall not be entitled to institute a suit...

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908 (Schedule 1), Section 9
Possible Sections.Full Text: The Courts shall... have jurisdiction to try all suits of a civil nature excepting suits of which their cognizance is either expressly or impliedly barred.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908 (Schedule 1), Order IX, Rule 1
Possible Sections.Full Text: On the day fixed in the summons for the defendant to appear and answer, the parties shall be in attendance...

Relevant Section: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908 (Schedule 1), Section 10
Section Text: No Court shall proceed with the trial of any suit in which the matter in issue is also directly and substantially in issue in a previously instituted suit between the same parties, or between parties under whom they or any of them claim litigating under the same title where such suit is pending in the same or any other Court in Bangladesh...

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Identifying the statutory rule for staying a suit due to pending concurrent litigation (Res Sub Judice).

Rule.Section 10: This section mandates a stay of trial if an earlier suit on the same issue between the same parties is pending.
Rule.Section 11: This provision bars the trial of issues that have already been definitively decided in a former suit.
Rule.Section 12: This section prevents the filing of new suits when specific procedural rules already bar further litigation.
Rule.Section 9: This section defines the general jurisdiction of civil courts and notes that certain suits can be barred.
Rule.Order IX, Rule 1: This rule requires parties to attend the hearing but does not govern the stay of the trial itself.

Application.Section 10: The statute explicitly prohibits 'proceeding with the trial' if the matter is already being litigated in a previously instituted suit.
Application.Section 11: Res judicata applies to completed cases, whereas Section 10 applies to cases that are still 'pending'.
Application.Section 12: Barring the 'institution' of a suit is different from staying the 'trial' of an already instituted suit.
Application.Section 9: General jurisdiction principles are the foundation, but Section 10 is the specific rule for staying trials for pendency.
Application.Order IX, Rule 1: Physical attendance rules do not address the legal stay of proceedings based on other pending cases.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, section 10, a court is barred from proceeding with the trial of any suit if the matter in issue is already being litigated in a previously instituted suit between the same parties. This is known as the doctrine of res sub judice. Therefore, (D) The matter is also in issue in a previously instituted suit between the same parties is correct.

Question:
Which explanation to Section 11 of the CPC defines 'Constructive Res Judicata'?
(A) Explanation IV
(B) Explanation II
(C) Explanation VI
(D) Explanation III

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908 (Schedule 1), Section 11
Possible Sections.Full Text: No Court shall try any suit or issue in which the matter... has been... finally decided... Explanation IV.**—Any matter which might and ought to have been made ground of defence or attack...

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908 (Schedule 1), Section 10
Possible Sections.Full Text: No Court shall proceed with the trial of any suit in which the matter in issue is also directly and substantially in issue in a previously instituted suit...

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908 (Schedule 1), Section 13
Possible Sections.Full Text: A foreign judgment shall be conclusive as to any matter thereby directly adjudicated upon between the same parties...

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908 (Schedule 1), Section 2(2)
Possible Sections.Full Text: "decree" means the formal expression of an adjudication... conclusively determines the rights of the parties...

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908 (Schedule 1), Order II, Rule 2
Possible Sections.Full Text: Every suit shall include the whole of the claim which the plaintiff is entitled to make in respect of the cause of action...

Relevant Section: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908 (Schedule 1), Section 11
Section Text: Explanation IV.**—Any matter which might and ought to have been made ground of defence or attack in such former suit shall be deemed to have been a matter directly and substantially in issue in such suit.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Identifying the specific statutory explanation that establishes the doctrine of constructive res judicata.

Rule.Section 11: This section prevents the re-litigation of decided matters and includes the rule for issues that 'ought' to have been raised.
Rule.Section 10: This provision governs the stay of trials for identical suits that are currently pending in other courts.
Rule.Section 13: This section outlines the rules for the finality and conclusiveness of judgments issued by foreign courts.
Rule.Section 2(2): This subsection provides the formal definition of a decree as a conclusive determination of party rights.
Rule.Order II, Rule 2: This rule prevents plaintiffs from splitting claims but is distinct from the res judicata doctrine for defenses.

Application.Section 11: Explanation IV specifically deems matters that 'might and ought' to have been raised as having been decided.
Application.Section 10: Staying a pending case for 'res sub judice' does not involve the finality of unraised defenses.
Application.Section 13: Foreign judgment rules focus on jurisdictional validity rather than the internal scope of omitted domestic pleadings.
Application.Section 2(2): General definitions of adjudications do not specify the individual rules for deemed issues in litigation.
Application.Order II, Rule 2: While similar in effect, Order II Rule 2 focuses on the 'plaintiff's whole claim' rather than all 'grounds of attack or defense'.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, section 11, Explanation IV, any matter which might and ought to have been made a ground of defense or attack in a former suit is deemed to have been decided. This is the doctrine of constructive res judicata. Therefore, (A) Explanation IV is correct.

Question:
Under Section 34 of the CPC, if a decree for payment of money is silent regarding further interest from the date of the decree, the Court is deemed to have —

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Identifying the legal presumption when a money decree omits mention of post-judgment interest.

Rule.Section 34: This section governs the award of interest in money decrees and specifies the consequence of court silence.
Rule.Section 35: This provision establishes the court's power to award interest on suit costs, capped at six percent.
Rule.Section 11: Explanation V of this section creates a general presumption that ungranted reliefs are deemed refused for res judicata.
Rule.Section 2(2): This subsection defines the nature of a decree as a final and formal determination of controversy.
Rule.Order XX, Rule 3: This rule prohibits the alteration of a signed judgment, emphasizing the finality of the written decree.

Application.Section 34: Subsection (2) explicitly states that silence in the decree regarding further interest is 'deemed to have refused' that interest.
Application.Section 35: Interest on costs is a separate category from interest on the principal decretal amount governed by Section 34.
Application.Section 11: While Explanation V supports the logic, Section 34(2) is the specific and conclusive provision for money interest.
Application.Section 2(2): The definition of a decree does not provide specific interpretive rules for silent interest clauses.
Application.Order XX, Rule 3: The inability to add to a signed judgment reinforces the finality of a 'deemed refusal' in the original text.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, section 34, Subsection (2), if a money decree is silent regarding further interest from the date of the decree to the date of payment, the Court is legally deemed to have refused such interest. Question:
According to Section 80 of the CPC, what is the mandatory notice period before instituting a suit against the Government?
(A) Six months
(B) Three months
(C) Two months
(D) Thirty days

Application.Section 80: Subsection (1) explicitly requires the expiration of 'two months' after notice before a suit can be instituted.

Question:

Application.Section 93: While the Collector can act, it is only as a surrogate for the Attorney General's primary power described in Section 91.
Application.Section 79: Naming the state as a party is a formal rule that does not define the standing for a community-based injury suit.
Application.Section 2(7): A Government Pleader is an appointee but does not possess the inherent threshold authority of the Attorney General for Section 91.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, section 91, Subsection (1), a suit for public nuisance can be instituted by the Attorney General. Alternatively, two or more persons can sue if they obtain the Attorney General's written consent. Therefore, (A) The Attorney General, or two or more persons with his written consent is correct.

Question:
What is the maximum compensation a defendant can be awarded for an arrest or attachment on insufficient grounds under Section 95 of the CPC?
(A) Fifty thousand Taka
(B) Ten thousand Taka
(C) One lakh Taka
(D) Five thousand Taka

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908 (Schedule 1), Section 95
Possible Sections.Full Text: award against the plaintiff... such amount, not exceeding ten thousand Taka, as it deems a reasonable compensation to the defendant...

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908 (Schedule 1), Section 35A
Possible Sections.Full Text: the Court shall... make an order for the payment... such cost by way of compensation which may... extend upto fifty thousand taka.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908 (Schedule 1), Section 35B
Possible Sections.Full Text: such application or written objection... shall not be admitted for hearing without payment by that party... not exceeding two thousand taka.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908 (Schedule 1), Section 32
Possible Sections.Full Text: The Court may compel the attendance of any person... (c) impose a fine upon him not exceeding five hundred Taka;

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908 (Schedule 1), Section 58
Possible Sections.Full Text: where the decree is for the payment of a sum of money exceeding fifty Taka, for a period of six months...

Relevant Section: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908 (Schedule 1), Section 95
Section Text: award against the plaintiff by its order such amount, not exceeding ten thousand Taka, as it deems a reasonable compensation to the defendant for the expense or injury caused to him: Provided that a Court shall not award, under this section, an amount exceeding the limits of its pecuniary jurisdiction.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Identifying the statutory monetary limit for compensation for wrongful interlocutory orders.

Rule.Section 95: This section limits compensation for wrongful arrest, attachment, or injunction to ten thousand Taka.
Rule.Section 35A: This provision caps compensatory costs for false or vexatious claims at fifty thousand Taka.
Rule.Section 35B: This section restricts the penalty for procedural filing delays to two thousand Taka.
Rule.Section 32: This provision sets the maximum fine for a witness's non-attendance at five hundred Taka.
Rule.Section 58: This section establishes the six-month duration for civil detention when the debt exceeds fifty Taka.

Application.Section 95: Subsection (1) explicitly provides the cap of 'ten thousand Taka' for compensation awarded to the aggrieved defendant.
Application.Section 35A: While involving compensation, Section 35A addresses the overall merits of the suit rather than interim wrongful orders.
Application.Section 35B: Costs for delays are specific procedural penalties and not a remedy for the harm of wrongful arrest.
Application.Section 32: Fines against witnesses go to the state, while Section 95 awards go to the injured party.
Application.Section 58: The fifty Taka threshold determines imprisonment time, not a compensation amount for the defendant's injury.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, section 95, Subsection (1), the Court may award a defendant up to ten thousand Taka in compensation if it finds an arrest or attachment was obtained on insufficient grounds. Therefore, (B) Ten thousand Taka is correct.

Possible Sections.Full Text: any person considering himself aggrieved... by a decree or order... from which no appeal is allowed... may apply for a view of judgment to the Court which passed the decree...

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908 (Schedule 1), Section 107
Possible Sections.Full Text: Subject to such conditions and limitations as may be prescribed, an Appellate Court shall have power- (a) to determine a case finally; (b) to remand a case...

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908 (Schedule 1), Section 99
Possible Sections.Full Text: No decree shall be reserved or substantially varied... in appeal on account of any misjoinder... not affecting the merits of the case or the jurisdiction...

Relevant Section: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908 (Schedule 1), Section 115
Section Text: The High Court Division may... call for the record... and if such Court appears to have committed any error of law resulting in an error in such decree or order occasioning failure of justice, the High Court Division may, revise such decree or order...

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Identifying the specific legal threshold for the High Court to exercise its revisional jurisdiction.

Rule.Section 115: This section defines the power of revision for cases where an error of law leads to a failure of justice.
Rule.Section 114: This provision establishes the remedy of review, allowing the original court to reconsider its own decision.
Rule.Section 113: This section provides for the mechanism of reference, where a lower court seeks a legal opinion from the High Court.
Rule.Section 107: This provision outlines the primary powers of an appellate court, including the power to remand or decide finally.
Rule.Section 99: This section prohibits appellate courts from reversing decrees for technical errors that do not affect the merits.

Application.Section 115: The text explicitly requires an 'error of law' resulting in an erroneous decision and a 'failure of justice' for a revision.
Application.Section 114: Review is a different remedy based on the discovery of new evidence or errors apparent on the face of the record.
Application.Section 113: Reference is a proactive request from the court itself, while revision is a challenge brought by an aggrieved party.
Application.Section 107: General appellate powers apply to appealable cases, whereas revision is specifically for orders where 'no appeal lies'.
Application.Section 99: While both emphasize justice over technicalities, Section 115 is the specific source for the High Court's revisional authority.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, section 115, Subsection (1), the High Court Division can revise a decree or order from a subordinate court if an error of law was committed. This error must have resulted in an erroneous decision and occasioned a failure of justice. Therefore, (D) Committed an error of law resulting in an erroneous decision occasioning failure of justice is correct.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Schedule II, Entry for Section 161, a public servant convicted of taking an illegal gratification faces a maximum penalty of imprisonment of either description for 3 years, or fine, or both. Rule.Schedule II, Entry 143: This section classifies unlawful assembly as a cognizable offence allowing arrest without a warrant.

Application.Schedule II, Entry 153A: This entry shares the non-cognizable status but applies to class enmity rather than sedition against the state.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Schedule II, Entry for Section 124A, sedition is categorized as a non-cognizable offence. IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Schedule II, Entry for Section 147, the offence of rioting is compoundable. Question:
The Evidence Act, 1872, came into force on —
(A) 1st September, 1872
(B) 1st July, 1872
(C) 1st January, 1872
(D) 1st May, 1872

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Evidence Act, 1872, Section 1
Possible Sections.Full Text: This act may be called the Evidence Act, 1872. It extends to the whole of Bangladesh and applies to all judicial proceedings in or before any Court, including Courts-martial, other than Courts-martial convened under the Army Act, 1952, the Naval Discipline Ordinance, 1961 or the Air Force Act, 1953 but not to affidavits presented to any Court or officer, nor to proceedings before an arbitrator; And it shall come into force on the first day of September, 1872.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Evidence Act, 1872, Section 3
Possible Sections.Full Text: In this Act the following words and expressions are used in the following senses, unless a contrary intention appears from the context:- "Court" includes all Judges and Magistrates and all persons, except arbitrators, legally authorized to take evidence. "Fact" means and includes- (1) anything, state of things, or relation of things capable of being perceived by the senses; (2) any mental condition of which any person is conscious.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Evidence Act, 1872, Section 4
Possible Sections.Full Text: **"May presume"** Whenever it is provided by this Act that the Court may presume a fact, it may either regard such fact as proved, unless and until it is disproved, or may call for proof of it: **“Shall presume”** Whenever it is directed by this Act that the Court shall presume a fact, it shall regard such fact as proved, unless and until it is disproved: **“Conclusive proof”** When one fact is declared by this Act to be conclusive proof of another, the Court shall, on proof of the one fact, regard the other as proved, and shall not allow evidence to be given for the purpose of disproving it.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Evidence Act, 1872, Section 5
Possible Sections.Full Text: Evidence may be given in any suit or proceeding of the existence or non-existence of every fact in issue and of such other fact as are hereinafter declared to be relevant, and of no others.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Evidence Act, 1872, Section 17
Possible Sections.Full Text: An admission is a statement, oral or documentary [or contained in digital record], which suggests any inference as to any fact in issue or relevant fact, and which is made by any of the persons, and under the circumstances, hereinafter mentioned.

Section Text: "Evidence" means and includes- (1) all statements which the Court permits or requires to be made before it by witnesses, in relation to matters of fact under inquiry: such statements are called oral evidence; (2) all documents produced for the inspection of the Court; such documents are called documentary evidence. (3) all materials or objects relating to blood, semen, hair, all body material, organ or part of organ, Deoxyribo Nucleic Acid (DNA), finger impression, palm impression, iris impression and foot print or any other similar material or object which may- (i) establish that an offence has been committed or establish a link or relation between an offence and its victim or an offence and its offender; and (ii) prove or disprove a fact: such materials or objects are called physical or forensic evidence.

Rule.Section 32: This section makes statements by deceased or unavailable persons relevant under specific circumstances.

Application.Section 3: The definition in Section 3 explicitly includes witnesses, documents, and physical/forensic materials like DNA, confirming option B.
Application.Section 4: Presumption rules determine how evidence is treated rather than defining what the evidence is itself.
Application.Section 5: The mandate for relevant facts describes what *can* be evidenced, not the physical nature of evidence.
Application.Section 32: Hearsay exceptions are a specialized category of relevancy rather than the primary definition of evidence.
Application.Section 41: Judgments are a form of proof but are categorized under relevancy rather than the basic definition of evidence.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 3, 'Evidence' is defined broadly to include three main categories: oral evidence from witness statements, documentary evidence, and physical or forensic evidence such as blood, DNA, or fingerprints. This comprehensive definition ensures all forms of proof are admissible. Therefore, (C) Witness statements, documents, and physical/forensic objects is correct.

Question:
Which of the following is NOT a 'document' under the Evidence Act, 1872?
(A) A map or plan
(B) An oral statement of a witness
(C) A caricature
(D) An inscription on a metal plate

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Evidence Act, 1872, Section 1
Possible Sections.Full Text: This act may be called the Evidence Act, 1872... it shall come into force on the first day of September, 1872.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Evidence Act, 1872, Section 3
Possible Sections.Full Text: "Document" means any matter expressed or described upon any substance by means of letters, figures or marks... A caricature is a document.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Evidence Act, 1872, Section 22
Possible Sections.Full Text: Oral admissions as to the contents of a document are not relevant, unless and until that the party proposing to prove them shows that he is entitled to give secondary evidence...

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Evidence Act, 1872, Section 32
Possible Sections.Full Text: Statements, written or verbal, of relevant facts made by a person who is dead, or who cannot be found... are themselves relevant facts...

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Evidence Act, 1872, Section 35
Possible Sections.Full Text: An entry in any public or other official book, register or record... stating a fact in issue or relevant fact... is itself a relevant fact.

Section Text: "Document" means any matter expressed or described upon any substance by means of letters, figures or marks, or by more than one of those means, intended to be used, or which may be used, for the purpose of recording that matter [and includes any digital record]. A writing is a document: Words printed, lithographed or photographed are documents: A map or plan is a document: An inscription on a metal plate or stone is a document: A caricature is a document.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: What is excluded from the statutory definition of a 'document' under the Evidence Act?

Rule.Section 1: This section provides the name, extent, and commencement of the Act.
Rule.Section 3: The interpretation clause defines 'document' as matter expressed on a substance by letters, figures, or marks.
Rule.Section 22: This rule determines when oral admissions regarding document contents are considered relevant.
Rule.Section 32: This section allows for the relevancy of statements by deceased or missing persons.
Rule.Section 35: This provision makes entries in official books or records relevant facts when made in performance of duty.

Application.Section 1: Territorial details are irrelevant to defining what physical objects constitute documents.
Application.Section 3: The definition explicitly lists caricatures, maps, and metal inscriptions as documents, but excludes oral statements, confirming option D.
Application.Section 22: This section treats documents as distinct from oral statements, reinforcing that they are different evidentiary categories.
Application.Section 32: This section distinguishes between written and verbal statements, noting that verbal statements are not documents.
Application.Section 35: Public records are a type of document, but this section focuses on their relevancy rather than defining the term.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 3, the term 'document' refers to matter expressed on any substance through letters, marks, or figures. While maps, caricatures, and inscriptions are documents, an oral statement by a witness is categorized as oral evidence. Therefore, (B) An oral statement of a witness is correct.

Question:

Rule.Section 34: This provision includes digital records within the relevancy rules for business account entries.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 3, 'Digital record' is defined as any information or data generated, sent, or stored in magnetic, electro-magnetic, optical, or computer memory formats. Question:
A fact is said to be ________ when, after considering the matters before it, the Court either believes it to exist, or considers its existence so probable that a prudent man ought to act upon the supposition that it exists.
(A) Proved
(B) Not proved
(C) Disproved
(D) Relevant

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Evidence Act, 1872, Section 3
Possible Sections.Full Text: **Proved** A fact is said to be proved when, after considering the matters before it, the Court either believes it to exist, or considers its existence so probable that a prudent man ought, under the circumstances of the particular case, to act upon the supposition that it exists.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Evidence Act, 1872, Section 4
Possible Sections.Full Text: **"May presume"** Whenever it is provided by this Act that the Court may presume a fact, it may either regard such fact as proved, unless and until it is disproved...

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Evidence Act, 1872, Section 11
Possible Sections.Full Text: Facts not otherwise relevant are relevant– (1) If they are inconsistent with any fact in issue or relevant fact; (2) If by themselves or in connection with other facts they make the existence or non-existence of any fact in issue or relevant fact highly probable or improbable.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Evidence Act, 1872, Section 31
Possible Sections.Full Text: Admissions are not conclusive proof of the matters admitted but they may operate as estoppels under the provisions hereinafter contained.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Evidence Act, 1872, Section 41
Possible Sections.Full Text: ...Such judgment, order or decree is conclusive proof- that any legal character which it confers accrued at the time when such judgment... come into operation...

Section Text: **Proved** A fact is said to be proved when, after considering the matters before it, the Court either believes it to exist, or considers its existence so probable that a prudent man ought, under the circumstances of the particular case, to act upon the supposition that it exists.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: What is the statutory standard for a fact being legally considered 'proved' in a court of law?

Rule.Section 3: The interpretation clause defines 'Proved' based on the court's belief or the probability seen by a prudent man.
Rule.Section 4: This provision defines 'may presume', where a fact is treated as proved until disproved.
Rule.Section 11: This rule defines the relevancy of facts that make other facts highly probable or improbable.
Rule.Section 31: This section states that admissions serve as evidence but are not absolute conclusive proof.
Rule.Section 41: This provision lists specific judgments that serve as conclusive proof of legal status.

Application.Section 3: The statutory language in Section 3 matches the description in the question for the term 'Proved', confirming option B.
Application.Section 4: Presumptions are a method of reaching a 'proved' state but do not define the standard of proof itself.
Application.Section 11: Probability is a ground for relevancy, but Section 3 provides the ultimate standard for when that probability amounts to legal proof.
Application.Section 31: Rules on the finality of admissions distinguish between relevance and the status of being 'proved'.
Application.Section 41: Conclusive proof is a special category and doesn't define the general standard for all facts in issue.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 3, a fact is 'proved' when the court, after evaluating all matters, either believes it exists or finds its existence so probable that a prudent person would assume it exists. This established the legal threshold for civil and criminal trials. Therefore, (A) Proved is correct.

Question:
Under Section 4 of the Evidence Act, 1872, when the Act directs that the Court 'shall presume' a fact, the Court —
(A) May either regard it as proved or call for proof
(B) Must treat it as conclusive proof
(C) Shall regard it as proved unless and until it is disproved
(D) Must ignore it until evidence is given

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Evidence Act, 1872, Section 3

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Evidence Act, 1872, Section 4

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Evidence Act, 1872, Section 5
Possible Sections.Full Text: Evidence may be given in any suit or proceeding of the existence or non-existence of every fact in issue...

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Evidence Act, 1872, Section 6
Possible Sections.Full Text: Facts which, though not in issue, are so connected with a fact in issue as to form part of the same transaction, are relevant...

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Evidence Act, 1872, Section 14
Possible Sections.Full Text: Facts showing the existence of any state of mind... are relevant, when the existence of any such state of mind... is in issue or relevant.

Section Text: **“Shall presume”** Whenever it is directed by this Act that the Court shall presume a fact, it shall regard such fact as proved, unless and until it is disproved:

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: What is the mandatory judicial procedure when a statute directs that a court 'shall presume' a fact?

Rule.Section 3: The interpretation clause provides definitions for 'proved', 'disproved', and other key terms.
Rule.Section 4: This provision defines 'shall presume' as a mandate to treat a fact as proved unless disproved.
Rule.Section 5: This rule restricts evidence to facts in issue and relevant facts as defined by the Act.
Rule.Section 6: This section addresses the relevancy of connected facts in the same transaction.
Rule.Section 14: This section governs the relevancy of facts showing intention or state of mind.

Application.Section 3: Definitions of 'proved' do not explain the procedural requirement of the 'shall presume' directive.
Application.Section 4: The statutory text explicitly states the court 'shall regard such fact as proved, unless and until it is disproved', confirming option B.
Application.Section 5: General admissibility rules are separate from the specific treatment of mandatory presumptions.
Application.Section 6: Res gestae relevancy is an optional finding of fact, unlike the mandatory nature of a 'shall presume' clause.
Application.Section 14: Mental states are proved by evidence, whereas 'shall presume' dictates the starting point for certain legal facts.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 4, the directive 'shall presume' requires the court to treat a specific fact as proved immediately. Question:

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Evidence Act, 1872, Section 9
Possible Sections.Full Text: Facts necessary to explain or introduce a fact in issue or relevant fact... are relevant in so far as they are necessary for that purpose.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Evidence Act, 1872, Section 11
Possible Sections.Full Text: Facts not otherwise relevant are relevant– (1) If they are inconsistent with any fact in issue or relevant fact...

Rule.Section 5: This provision limits evidence to facts in issue and facts declared relevant by the Act.

Rule.Section 7: This provision outlines the relevancy of facts that serve as the occasion, cause, or effect of other facts.
Rule.Section 9: This section makes facts relevant if they explain or introduce relevant facts or establish identity.
Rule.Section 11: This rule applies to facts that are inconsistent with facts in issue or make them highly probable.

Application.Section 6: The statutory text explicitly declares facts relevant 'whether they occurred at the same time and place or at different times and places', confirming option A.
Application.Section 7: Cause and effect relevancy is a distinct category and does not govern the same-transaction rule.
Application.Section 9: Introduced facts are limited by necessity, whereas transaction facts are relevant by their inherent connection.
Application.Section 11: Alibi and probability rules are separate from the transactional connectivity described in Section 6.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 6, the doctrine of res gestae establishes that facts forming part of the same transaction are relevant. Application.Section 9: The relationship between conspirators is relevant, but Section 10 governs the admissibility of their words and deeds.

Application.Section 30: Confessions at trial are a separate procedural matter from statements made during the conspiracy's operation.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 10, once there is reasonable ground to believe a conspiracy exists, the words and actions of any member are relevant against all others. This relevancy begins from the moment the first member of the plot formed the criminal intention. Therefore, (C) The time when such intention was first entertained by any one of them is correct.

Question:

(B) Section 7
(C) Section 8

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Evidence Act, 1872, Section 7
Possible Sections.Full Text: Facts which are the occasions, cause or effect... of relevant facts... are relevant.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Evidence Act, 1872, Section 8
Possible Sections.Full Text: Any fact is relevant which shows or constitutes a motive or preparation...

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Evidence Act, 1872, Section 9
Possible Sections.Full Text: Facts necessary to explain... a fact in issue... or establish the identity... are relevant...

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Evidence Act, 1872, Section 11
Possible Sections.Full Text: Facts not otherwise relevant are relevant– (1) If they are inconsistent with any fact in issue or relevant fact; (2) If... they make the existence... highly probable or improbable.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Evidence Act, 1872, Section 14
Possible Sections.Full Text: Facts showing the existence of any state of mind... are relevant...

Section Text: Facts not otherwise relevant are relevant– (1) If they are inconsistent with any fact in issue or relevant fact;

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Which section of the Evidence Act makes the plea of alibi (inconsistency with being at the crime scene) relevant?

Rule.Section 7: This provision makes facts that are the occasion, cause, or effect of a fact in issue relevant.
Rule.Section 8: This section governs the relevancy of facts relating to motive, preparation, and conduct.
Rule.Section 9: This provision makes facts relevant if they explain or introduce relevant facts or identity.
Rule.Section 11: This section makes facts relevant if they are inconsistent with a fact in issue or make it highly improbable.
Rule.Section 14: This provision governs the relevancy of facts showing a specific state of mind or body.

Application.Section 7: An alibi is not a causal effect of a crime but a fact that negates the suspect's involvement.
Application.Section 8: Being elsewhere is a physical location fact rather than behavior or motive.
Application.Section 9: Alibi evidence is more than an introduction; it is a substantive fact that contradicts the prosecution's case.
Application.Section 11: The statutory text in Subsection 1 covers facts 'inconsistent' with facts in issue, which is the legal basis for an alibi, confirming option D.
Application.Section 14: Location at a certain time does not prove a state of mind like intent or knowledge.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 11, facts that are inconsistent with a fact in issue are relevant. A plea of alibi, where the accused claims to have been elsewhere, is relevant because it is inconsistent with the fact that they committed the crime. Therefore, (D) Section 11 is correct.

Question:
Under the Evidence Act, 1872, an 'admission' is a statement which suggests —
(A) The motive of the party
(B) Proof of a document's contents
(C) Any inference as to any fact in issue or relevant fact

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Evidence Act, 1872, Section 17
Possible Sections.Full Text: An admission is a statement, oral or documentary [or contained in digital record], which suggests any inference as to any fact in issue or relevant fact...

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Evidence Act, 1872, Section 18
Possible Sections.Full Text: Statements made by a party to the proceeding, or by an agent to any such party... are admissions.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Evidence Act, 1872, Section 21
Possible Sections.Full Text: Admissions are relevant and may be proved as against the person who makes them... but they can not be proved by or on behalf of the person...

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Evidence Act, 1872, Section 31
Possible Sections.Full Text: Admissions are not conclusive proof of the matters admitted but they may operate as estoppels...

Section Text: An admission is a statement, oral or documentary [or contained in digital record], which suggests any inference as to any fact in issue or relevant fact, and which is made by any of the persons, and under the circumstances, hereinafter mentioned.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: How is the term 'admission' defined and what is its evidentiary function under the Act?

Rule.Section 3: The interpretation clause provides the foundational definition of 'facts in issue'.
Rule.Section 17: This provision defines an admission as a statement suggesting an inference regarding a fact in issue or relevant fact.
Rule.Section 18: This section identifies the specific persons whose statements qualify as admissions, such as parties or agents.
Rule.Section 21: This rule determines the admissibility of admissions for or against the person making them.
Rule.Section 31: This provision clarifies that admissions are not final proof but can create an estoppel.

Application.Section 3: Defining 'facts in issue' provides context for admissions but is not the definition of the term itself.
Application.Section 17: The statutory text explicitly defines an admission as suggesting 'any inference as to any fact in issue or relevant fact', confirming option B.
Application.Section 18: This section limits *who* can make an admission but doesn't define the *nature* of the statement.
Application.Section 21: Rules for proof follow the definition of the term and do not provide the initial meaning.
Application.Section 31: The legal effect of an admission is a separate concept from its formal statutory definition.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 17, an 'admission' is defined as any statement—whether oral, documentary, or digital—that suggests an inference about a fact in issue or a relevant fact. Admissions are key pieces of evidence provided they are made by authorized persons. Therefore, (C) Any inference as to any fact in issue or relevant fact is correct.

Question:
A confession made by an accused is irrelevant if it is caused by —
(A) Being in a state of drunkenness
(B) Inducement, threat or promise from a person in authority
(C) Deception
(D) A promise of secrecy

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Evidence Act, 1872, Section 24
Possible Sections.Full Text: A confession made by an accused person is irrelevant... if... caused by any inducement, threat or promise... proceeding from a person in authority...

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Evidence Act, 1872, Section 25
Possible Sections.Full Text: No confession made to a police-officer shall be proved as against a person accused of any offence.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Evidence Act, 1872, Section 28
Possible Sections.Full Text: If such a confession as is referred to in section 24 is made after the impression caused by any such inducement... has... been fully removed, it is relevant.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Evidence Act, 1872, Section 29
Possible Sections.Full Text: If such a confession is otherwise relevant, it does not become irrelevant merely because it was made under a promise of secrecy, or in consequence of a deception... or when he was drunk...

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Evidence Act, 1872, Section 30
Possible Sections.Full Text: When more persons than one are being tried jointly... a confession made by one... affecting himself and some other... may take into consideration...

Section Text: A confession made by an accused person is irrelevant in a criminal proceeding, if the making of the confession appears to the Court to have been caused by any inducement, threat or promise having reference to the charge against the accused person, proceeding from a person in authority and sufficient, in the opinion of the Court, to give the accused person grounds which would appear to him reasonable for supposing that by making it he would gain any advantage or avoid any evil of a temporal nature in reference to the proceedings against him.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Under what specific conditions of duress or influence is an accused's confession considered legally irrelevant?

Rule.Section 24: This provision makes confessions irrelevant if caused by inducement, threat, or promise from a person in authority.
Rule.Section 25: This rule creates an absolute bar against proving any confession made to a police officer.
Rule.Section 28: This section makes an induced confession relevant if the initial impression of duress has been removed.
Rule.Section 29: This provision clarifies that confessions are not irrelevant merely due to secrecy, deception, or drunkenness.
Rule.Section 30: This section permits the consideration of a joint confession against co-accused persons.

Application.Section 24: The statutory text explicitly identifies 'inducement, threat or promise' from authority as the ground for irrelevancy, confirming option C.
Application.Section 25: While police confessions are excluded, the specific ground of 'inducement' is handled by Section 24.
Application.Section 28: This rule applies only after the Section 24 conditions have ceased to exist.
Application.Section 29: The factors in options A, B, and D are specifically declared *not* to make a confession irrelevant, making them incorrect.
Application.Section 30: Rules for joint trials do not address the initial voluntariness or relevancy of the confession itself.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 24, a confession is legally irrelevant if it was obtained through inducement, threat, or a promise made by a person in authority. For the confession to be excluded, the accused must have believed they would gain a temporal advantage by confessing. Therefore, (B) Inducement, threat or promise from a person in authority is correct.

Question:
According to Section 25 of the Evidence Act, 1872, a confession made to a ________ shall NOT be proved as against an accused.

(B) Physician
(C) Magistrate
(D) Clergyman

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Evidence Act, 1872, Section 25
Possible Sections.Full Text: No confession made to a police-officer shall be proved as against a person accused of any offence.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Evidence Act, 1872, Section 26
Possible Sections.Full Text: No confession made by any person whilst he is in the custody of a police-officer, unless it be made in the immediate presence of a Magistrate, shall be proved...

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Evidence Act, 1872, Section 27
Possible Sections.Full Text: Provided that, when any fact is deposed to as discovered in consequence of information received from a person accused... in the custody of a police officer... so much... may be proved.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Evidence Act, 1872, Section 29

Section Text: No confession made to a police-officer shall be proved as against a person accused of any offence.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: To which specific official is a confession barred from being proved under the Evidence Act?

Rule.Section 24: This provision makes confessions irrelevant if they are obtained through improper inducement or duress.
Rule.Section 25: This section creates a mandatory bar against proving any confession made to a police officer.
Rule.Section 26: This provision prohibits confessions made in police custody unless a Magistrate is present.
Rule.Section 27: This rule creates an exception for information that leads to the discovery of physical evidence.
Rule.Section 29: This provision clarifies that certain conditions like deception do not invalidate an otherwise relevant confession.

Application.Section 24: General rules on inducement are separate from the absolute prohibition involving police officers.
Application.Section 25: The statutory text explicitly prohibits confessions made to a 'police-officer' from being proved, confirming option B.
Application.Section 26: Confessions *in custody* follow a different rule, but Section 25 is the specific bar for confessions *to* the officer.
Application.Section 27: This section is a proviso that modifies the bars in sections 25 and 26 but does not define them.
Application.Section 29: The factors listed here relate to circumstances other than the identity of the person receiving the confession.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 25, a confession made to a police officer is completely inadmissible as evidence against an accused person. This absolute prohibition is intended to prevent police coercion and ensure that all confessions are made voluntarily before a magistrate. Therefore, (A) Police-officer is correct.

Question:

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Evidence Act, 1872, Section 24
Possible Sections.Full Text: A confession made by an accused person is irrelevant... if... caused by any inducement...

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Evidence Act, 1872, Section 25
Possible Sections.Full Text: No confession made to a police-officer shall be proved as against a person accused of any offence.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Evidence Act, 1872, Section 26
Possible Sections.Full Text: No confession made by any person whilst he is in the custody of a police-officer, unless it be made in the immediate presence of a Magistrate, shall be proved...

Rule.Section 24: This provision makes confessions irrelevant if they are obtained through improper inducement by authority.
Rule.Section 25: This rule creates an absolute bar against confessions made directly to police officers.
Rule.Section 26: This section prohibits confessions made in police custody unless a Magistrate is immediately present.
Rule.Section 27: This provision allows for the proof of information from an accused that leads to the discovery of a specific fact.
Rule.Section 32: This rule addresses the relevancy of dying declarations and other statements from unavailable witnesses.

Application.Section 24: General duress rules apply regardless of physical custody status.
Application.Section 25: Confessions to police are always barred, whereas Section 26 addresses the environment of custody.
Application.Section 26: The statutory text explicitly identifies 'the immediate presence of a Magistrate' as the only condition for admissibility during custody, confirming option C.
Application.Section 27: This section is a limited exception for physical discovery rather than the general rule for confessions.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 26, a confession made by a person in police custody is inadmissible unless it was made in the immediate presence of a Magistrate. Question:

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Evidence Act, 1872, Section 24
Possible Sections.Full Text: A confession made by an accused person is irrelevant... if... caused by any inducement...

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Evidence Act, 1872, Section 25
Possible Sections.Full Text: No confession made to a police-officer shall be proved as against a person accused of any offence.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Evidence Act, 1872, Section 26
Possible Sections.Full Text: No confession made by any person whilst he is in the custody of a police-officer... unless in the presence of a Magistrate...

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Evidence Act, 1872, Section 27
Possible Sections.Full Text: Provided that, when any fact is deposed to as discovered in consequence of information received from a person accused of any offence, in the custody of a police officer, so much of such information, whether it amounts to a confession or not, as relates distinctly to the fact thereby discovered, may be proved.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Evidence Act, 1872, Section 32
Possible Sections.Full Text: Statements... of relevant facts made by a person who is dead... are themselves relevant facts...

Section Text: Provided that, when any fact is deposed to as discovered in consequence of information received from a person accused of any offence, in the custody of a police officer, so much of such information, whether it amounts to a confession or not, as relates distinctly to the fact thereby discovered, may be proved.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: How much information from a custodial suspect is admissible if it leads the police to discover physical evidence?

Rule.Section 24: This provision excludes confessions obtained through improper inducement or threats.
Rule.Section 25: This rule bars the use of confessions made directly to police officers.
Rule.Section 26: This section prohibits confessions in police custody unless made before a Magistrate.
Rule.Section 27: This rule allows for the proof of any information that relates distinctly to a discovered physical fact.
Rule.Section 32: This provision governs the relevancy of statements by deceased persons as an exception to the hearsay rule.

Application.Section 24: Discovery rules are a specific exception that can apply even if other relevancy bars are present.
Application.Section 25: Section 27 acts as a proviso that overrides the general police confession bar in Section 25.
Application.Section 26: The custodial requirement for Section 27 is identical to Section 26, but the scope of proof is different.
Application.Section 27: The statutory text explicitly limits proof to 'so much... as relates distinctly to the fact thereby discovered', confirming option B.
Application.Section 32: Custodial discovery is a separate procedural event from the death of a witness.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 27, when information from an accused in custody leads to the discovery of a fact (like a hidden weapon), that specific portion of the statement is admissible. Only the part that relates distinctly to the discovery can be proved. Therefore, (B) Only so much as relates distinctly to the fact discovered is correct.

Question:

(B) Section 60
(C) Section 6

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Evidence Act, 1872, Section 6
Possible Sections.Full Text: Facts which, though not in issue, are so connected with a fact in issue as to form part of the same transaction, are relevant, whether they occurred at the same time and place or at different times and places.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Evidence Act, 1872, Section 32
Possible Sections.Full Text: Statements, written or verbal, of relevant facts made by a person who is dead, or who cannot be found, or who has become incapable of giving evidence... are themselves relevant facts in the following cases:– (1) When the statement is made by person as to the cause of his death...

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Evidence Act, 1872, Section 21
Possible Sections.Full Text: Admissions are relevant and may be proved as against the person who makes them, or his representative in interest; but they can not be proved by or on behalf of the person who makes them or by his representative in interest, except in the following cases...

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Evidence Act, 1872, Section 45
The Evidence Act, 1872, Section 33

Section Text: When the statement is made by person as to the cause of his death, or as to any of the circumstances of the transaction which resulted in his death, in cases in which the cause of that person's death comes into question. Such statements are relevant whether the person who made them was or was not, at the time when they were made, under expectation of death, and whatever may be the nature of the proceeding in which the cause of his death comes into question.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Which section of the Evidence Act makes a dying declaration relevant?

Rule.Section 6: This provision addresses facts that are part of the same transaction as a fact in issue.
Rule.Section 32: This section makes statements by deceased persons about the cause of their death relevant.
Rule.Section 21: This rule governs when admissions can be proved by or against the person making them.
Rule.Section 45: This rule (not provided) typically concerns the relevancy of expert opinions in specialized fields.
Rule.Section 33: This provision makes previous judicial testimony relevant if the witness is now unavailable.

Application.Section 6: Spontaneous declarations at the time of injury might fit here, but Section 32 is the specific rule for death causes.
Application.Section 32: Subsection (1) explicitly covers statements by a person regarding the cause of their own death, confirming option B.
Application.Section 21: A dying declaration is a hearsay exception for a third party, not an admission by a party to the suit.
Application.Section 45: The cause of death given by the victim is personal knowledge, not a professional expert's opinion.
Application.Section 33: Section 33 applies only to evidence given in a formal judicial capacity, not informal statements about death.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 32, Clause (1), a statement made by a person explaining the cause of their death or the circumstances of the fatal transaction is relevant. This applies in any proceeding where their death is an issue, regardless of their expectation of death. Therefore, (D) Section 32 is correct.

Question:

Rule.Section 35: This rule applies to the relevancy of official entries made by public servants.
Rule.Section 42: This provision addresses the relevancy of court judgments in specific categories.

Application.Section 13: Section 13 deals with acts and instances rather than the verbal or written opinions of individuals.

Section Text: An entry in any public or other official book, register or record [,or digital record], stating a fact in issue or relevant fact, and made by a public servant in the discharge of his official duty, or by any other person in performance of a duty specially enjoined by the law of the country in which such book, register [, record or digital record] is kept, is itself a relevant fact.

Question:
Which of the following is correct regarding presumptions under Section 4 of the Evidence Act, 1872?
(A) Presumptions are only used in criminal cases
(B) Conclusive proof allows evidence to be given to disprove the fact
(C) The Court has no discretion when the Act says it 'may presume' a fact
(D) When the Act directs the Court 'shall presume' a fact, the Court must regard it as proved unless disproved

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Evidence Act, 1872, Section 4
Possible Sections.Full Text: **"May presume"**... **“Shall presume”** Whenever it is directed by this Act that the Court shall presume a fact, it shall regard such fact as proved... **“Conclusive proof”**...

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Evidence Act, 1872, Section 3
Possible Sections.Full Text: **Proved** A fact is said to be proved when... **Disproved** A fact is said to be disproved when...

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Evidence Act, 1872, Section 31
Possible Sections.Full Text: Admissions are not conclusive proof... but they may operate as estoppels...

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Evidence Act, 1872, Section 41
Possible Sections.Full Text: A final judgment... is conclusive proof- that any legal character which it confers accrued...

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Evidence Act, 1872, Section 5
Possible Sections.Full Text: Evidence may be given... of every fact in issue... and of no others.

Section Text: **“Shall presume”** Whenever it is directed by this Act that the Court shall presume a fact, it shall regard such fact as proved, unless and until it is disproved: **“Conclusive proof”** When one fact is declared by this Act to be conclusive proof of another, the Court shall, on proof of the one fact, regard the other as proved, and shall not allow evidence to be given for the purpose of disproving it.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: What is the mandatory procedural requirement for a 'shall presume' directive in the Evidence Act?

Rule.Section 3: This interpretation clause defines the standards of 'proved' and 'disproved'.
Rule.Section 31: This section states that admissions are not conclusive proof.
Rule.Section 41: This provision identifies specialized judgments that act as conclusive proof of status.
Rule.Section 5: This rule restricts evidence to facts in issue and relevant facts.

Application.Section 4: The statute explicitly states that for 'shall presume', the court 'shall regard such fact as proved, unless and until it is disproved', confirming option B.
Application.Section 3: General definitions of proof do not mandate the specific behavior for the 'shall presume' instruction.
Application.Section 31: The lack of conclusive proof in admissions is the opposite of the conclusive proof rules in Section 4.
Application.Section 41: Specialized conclusive proof in Section 41 is a subset of the general definitions found in Section 4.
Application.Section 5: Admissibility limits do not describe the procedural handling of legal presumptions.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 4, the law defines different levels of presumptions. When the Act uses the directive 'shall presume', the court is required to treat that fact as proved unless evidence is presented that disproves it. Therefore, (D) When the Act directs the Court 'shall presume' a fact, the Court must regard it as proved unless disproved is correct.

Question:

Section Text: “good faith”: nothing shall be deemed to be done in good faith which is not done with due care and attention:

Rule.Section 4: This provision allows for filing on the next working day if a deadline falls on a holiday.
Rule.Section 2: The definitions section provides that good faith requires the exercise of due care and attention.
Rule.Section 5: This rule permits courts to condone delays in appeals if sufficient cause is shown by the party.
Rule.Section 6: This provision outlines the extension of limitation periods for persons suffering from legal disabilities like minority.

Application.Section 4: The closure of the court is a physical circumstance unrelated to the definition of a party's intent.

Application.Section 6: Legal disability concerns the capacity of a person to sue rather than the definition of their conduct's intent.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Limitation Act, 1908, section 2, Subsection (7), no act is deemed to be performed in good faith unless it is carried out with due care and attention. This statutory definition emphasizes the necessity of diligence rather than mere honest intention when determining legal good faith. Therefore, (A) Due care and attention is correct.

Question:
