(B) Section 6
(C) Section 8
(D) Section 7

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Section 6
Possible Sections.Full Text: Where a person entitled to institute a suit or proceeding or make an application for the execution of a decree is, at the time from which the period of limitation is to be reckoned, a minor, or insane, or an idiot, he may institute the suit or proceeding or make the application within the same period after the disability has ceased, as would otherwise have been allowed from the time prescribed therefore in the third column of the first schedule or in section 48 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Section 7
Possible Sections.Full Text: Where one of several persons jointly entitled to institute a suit or proceeding or make an application for the execution of a decree is under any such disability, and discharge can be given without the concurrence of such person, time will run against them all: but, where no such discharge can be given, time will not run as against any of them until one of them becomes capable of giving such discharge without the concurrence of the others or until the disability has ceased.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Section 8
Possible Sections.Full Text: Nothing in section 6 or in section 7 applies to suits to enforce rights of pre-emption, or shall be deemed to extend, for more than three years from the cessation of the disability or the death of the person affected thereby, the period within which any suit must be instituted or application made.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Section 9
Possible Sections.Full Text: Where once time has begun to run, no subsequent disability or inability to sue stops it: Provided that where letters of administration to the estate of a creditor have been granted to his debtor, the running of the time prescribed for a suit to recover the debt shall be suspended while the administration continues.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Section 10
Possible Sections.Full Text: Notwithstanding anything hereinbefore contained, no suit against a person in whom property has become vested in trust for any specific purpose, or against his legal representatives or assigns (not being assigns for valuable consideration), for the purpose of following in his or their hands such property or the proceeds thereof, or for an account of such property or proceeds, shall be barred by any length of time.

Section Text: Where a person entitled to institute a suit or proceeding or make an application for the execution of a decree is, at the time from which the period of limitation is to be reckoned, a minor, or insane, or an idiot, he may institute the suit or proceeding or make the application within the same period after the disability has ceased, as would otherwise have been allowed from the time prescribed therefore in the third column of the first schedule or in section 48 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Which specific section of the Limitation Act establishes the general rule for legal disability?

Rule.Section 6: This section allows minors, insane persons, or idiots to file suits after their disability ceases.

Rule.Section 8: This provision sets a three-year limit on extensions granted under the disability sections.
Rule.Section 9: This section mandates that once time begins to run, no subsequent disability can stop it.
Rule.Section 10: This rule specifies that suits against express trustees are never barred by the passage of time.

Application.Section 6: The primary provision for legal disability for an individual is contained in Section 6, making option A correct.
Application.Section 7: Disability among joint parties is a specific subset of the general rule and does not define the concept.
Application.Section 8: Setting exceptions and caps on the extension does not constitute the primary provision for the disability itself.
Application.Section 9: Continuous running of time is a separate principle that limits when disability can be pleaded for extension.
Application.Section 10: Exempting trustees from limitation is a category of suit, not a rule regarding the plaintiff's personal disability.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Limitation Act, 1908, section 6, Subsection (1), persons such as minors, the insane, or idiots are granted an extension of the limitation period. They may institute a suit after their disability has ceased within the same timeframe originally prescribed. Therefore, (B) Section 6 is correct.

Question:
A firm is owed a debt by A. The partners are B, C, and D. B is insane and C is a minor, but D is capable of giving a discharge without their concurrence. When does time run against them?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Where one of several persons jointly entitled to institute a suit or proceeding or make an application for the execution of a decree is under any such disability, and discharge can be given without the concurrence of such person, time will run against them all: but, where no such discharge can be given, time will not run as against any of them until one of them becomes capable of giving such discharge without the concurrence of the others or until the disability has ceased.

Rule.Section 21: This section clarifies that joint partners are not automatically liable for each other's written acknowledgments.

Question:

Application.Section 18: The statutory language explicitly measures time from when the fraud first became known to the injured person, confirming B.
Application.Section 19: Acknowledgments extend time for known liabilities, whereas fraud deals with the initial discovery of the right.

Application.Section 21: The definition of an agent does not alter the starting point for limitation in cases of fraud.

Question:
In the case of a continuing breach of contract, when does a fresh period of limitation begin to run?

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Section 19

Application.Section 25: Calendar standards are for computation methods but do not specify the triggering events for limitation.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Limitation Act, 1908, section 23, in cases of a continuing breach of contract, a fresh period of limitation begins to run at every moment the breach continues. Question:
A right to sue as a landlord to recover possession from a tenant accrues to A, who is an idiot. A dies three years after the accruer while still an idiot. A's representative has nine years under ordinary law. Section 6 of The Limitation Act, 1908 will:

Possible Sections.Full Text: Where one of several persons jointly entitled to institute a suit... is under any such disability, and discharge can be given without the concurrence... time will run against them all.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Section 9
Possible Sections.Full Text: Where once time has begun to run, no subsequent disability or inability to sue stops it.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Section 17
Possible Sections.Full Text: Where person against whom, if he were living, a right to institute a suit or make an application would have accrued dies before the right accrues.

Rule.Section 9: This provision mandates the continuous running of time once it has initially begun.
Rule.Section 17: This rule applies when a right to sue would have accrued but the person dies before that accrual.

Application.Section 8: Illustration (c) confirms that since the representative already has nine years (more than the three-year cap in Section 8), no further extension is given, confirming B.
Application.Section 7: The scenario involves an individual idiot, not joint creditors requiring shared discharge rules.
Application.Section 9: Since the disability existed at accrual, the 'continuous running' rule does not immediately bar the representative's claim.
Application.Section 17: The right accrued *before* A's death, so the 'death before accrual' rules of Section 17 are inapplicable.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Limitation Act, 1908, section 8, Illustration (c), if the ordinary limitation period remaining after a disabled person's death already exceeds three years, Section 6 provides no additional extension. The law only extends time if the remaining period is less than three years. Therefore, (D) Not extend that time is correct.

Rule.Section 3: This rule lists suits, appeals, and applications as separate categories for the purpose of dismissal.
Rule.Section 5: This section provides a remedy for delays in appeals and applications but omits suits.
Rule.Section 4: This provision applies to suits, appeals, and applications when a filing deadline falls on a holiday.

Application.Section 1: General introductory details do not provide the specific exclusions for legal terminology like 'suit'.
Application.Section 2: Subsection 10 explicitly excludes appeals and applications from the definition of a suit, confirming option C.
Application.Section 3: The fact that Section 3 lists them separately supports the definition that they are not the same thing.
Application.Section 5: The availability of Section 5 for appeals but not for suits confirms their distinct legal status.
Application.Section 4: Including all three terms in the holiday rule highlights their separation in the eyes of the law.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Limitation Act, 1908, section 2, Subsection (10), the definition of a "suit" is restricted. It explicitly excludes an appeal or an application. This distinction is crucial because different sections of the Act apply to suits compared to other proceedings. Therefore, (D) An appeal or application is correct.

Question:
Under Section 17 of the Limitation Act, 1908, if a person against whom a right to sue would have accrued dies before the right accrues, time runs from when there is a legal representative:
(A) Of the deceased against whom the plaintiff may sue
(B) Appointed by the District Judge
(C) Of the plaintiff capable of suing
(D) Capable of managing the estate

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Section 17
Possible Sections.Full Text: Where person against whom, if he were living, a right to institute a suit or make an application would have accrued dies before the right accrues, the period of limitation shall be computed from the time when there is a legal representative of the deceased against whom the plaintiff may institute or make such suit or application.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Section 18
Possible Sections.Full Text: Where any person having a right to institute a suit or make an application has, by means of fraud, been kept from the knowledge of such right or of the title on which it is founded.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Section 19
Possible Sections.Full Text: Where, before the expiration of the period prescribed for a suit or application in respect of any property or right, an acknowledgement of liability in respect of such property or right has been made in writing.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Section 9
Possible Sections.Full Text: Where once time has begun to run, no subsequent disability or inability to sue stops it.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Section 6
Possible Sections.Full Text: Where the disability continues up to the death of such person, his legal representative may institute the suit or make the application within the same period after the death.

Section Text: Where person against whom, if he were living, a right to institute a suit or make an application would have accrued dies before the right accrues, the period of limitation shall be computed from the time when there is a legal representative of the deceased against whom the plaintiff may institute or make such suit or application.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: How is limitation computed when the potential defendant dies before the right to sue them matures?

Rule.Section 17: This provision postpones limitation until there is a legal representative of the deceased defendant whom the plaintiff can sue.
Rule.Section 18: This rule postpones limitation in cases where a right has been concealed by fraud.
Rule.Section 19: This section computes a fresh period of limitation from a signed written acknowledgment of liability.
Rule.Section 9: This rule mandates the continuous running of time once it has initially begun, despite later disabilities.
Rule.Section 6: This provision allows the legal representative of a deceased *plaintiff* to sue after the plaintiff's death.

Application.Section 17: Subsection 2 specifically addresses the death of the potential defendant, starting time once a representative exists to be sued, confirming B.
Application.Section 18: Fraud-based concealment is not relevant to the natural event of a party's death before accrual.
Application.Section 19: A fresh period from acknowledgment requires a voluntary statement, unlike the procedural wait for a representative.
Application.Section 9: The continuous running rule doesn't apply because the clock hasn't started yet due to the lack of a defendant.
Application.Section 6: Section 6 focuses on the disability of the *plaintiff*, not the death of the *defendant* before accrual.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Limitation Act, 1908, section 17, Subsection (2), if a potential defendant dies before the right to sue them accrues, limitation only begins when a legal representative of the deceased exists against whom the suit can be brought. Therefore, (A) Of the deceased against whom the plaintiff may sue is correct.

Question:
Under the Limitation Act, 1908, the time during which a defendant has been absent from Bangladesh shall be ________ in computing the period of limitation.
(A) Suspended for three years
(B) Ignored
(C) Excluded
(D) Counted

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Section 12
Possible Sections.Full Text: In computing the period of limitation prescribed for any suit, appeal or application, the day from which such period is to be reckoned shall be excluded.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Section 13
Possible Sections.Full Text: In computing the period of limitation prescribed for any suit, the time during which the defendant has been absent from Bangladesh and from the territories beyond Bangladesh under the administration of the Government shall be excluded.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Section 11
Possible Sections.Full Text: Suits instituted in Bangladesh on contracts entered into in a foreign country are subject to the rules of limitation contained in this Act.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Section 14
Possible Sections.Full Text: In computing the period of limitation prescribed for any suit, the time during which the plantiff has been prosecuting with due diligence another civil proceeding... shall be excluded.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Section 15
Possible Sections.Full Text: In computing the period of limitation prescribed for any suit... the institution or execution of which has been stayed by injunction or order... shall be excluded.

Section Text: In computing the period of limitation prescribed for any suit, the time during which the defendant has been absent from Bangladesh and from the territories beyond Bangladesh under the administration of the Government shall be excluded.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is the period of a defendant's absence from the country included or excluded from limitation calculations?

Rule.Section 12: This rule provides for the exclusion of the day of reckoning and document-acquisition time.
Rule.Section 13: This section mandates the exclusion of the time during which a defendant is absent from Bangladesh.
Rule.Section 11: This provision subjects foreign contracts to local limitation rules when sued upon in Bangladesh.
Rule.Section 14: This rule excludes time spent litigating in a court that lacked jurisdiction.
Rule.Section 15: This section excludes time when a suit or execution is stayed by a court-issued injunction.

Application.Section 12: Excluding the starting day is a standard computation rule unrelated to the party's physical location.
Application.Section 13: The statute explicitly states that the time of the defendant's absence shall be excluded, confirming option B.
Application.Section 11: Rules for foreign contracts govern the 'what' law applies, but Section 13 governs the 'how long' calculation.
Application.Section 14: Time lost in an incompetent court is a procedural exclusion rather than a geographic one.
Application.Section 15: A stay of execution is a judicial halt, whereas Section 13 is based on the physical presence of the defendant.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Limitation Act, 1908, section 13, the period during which a defendant is physically absent from Bangladesh must be excluded when computing the limitation for any suit. This ensures that the plaintiff is not penalized for the defendant's unavailability. Therefore, (C) Excluded is correct.

Question:
Regarding easement rights under Section 26 of the Limitation Act, 1908, an "interruption" is only valid if it is submitted to or acquiesced in for ________ after the claimant has notice thereof.
(A) Two years
(B) Three years
(C) Six months
(D) One year

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Section 26
Possible Sections.Full Text: Nothing is an interruption within the meaning of this section, unless where there is an actual discontinuance of the possession or enjoyment by reason of an obstruction by the act of some person other than the claimant, and unless such obstruction is submitted to or acquiesced in for one year after the claimant has notice thereof.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Section 27
Possible Sections.Full Text: Where any land or water upon, over or from which any easement has been enjoyed... has been held under or by virtue of any interest for life... the time of the enjoyment of such easement during the continuance of such interest or term shall be excluded.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Section 28
Possible Sections.Full Text: At the determination of the period hereby limited to any person for instituting a suit for possession of any property, his right to such property shall be extinguished.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Section 25
Possible Sections.Full Text: All instruments shall, for the purposes of this Act, be deemed to be made with reference to the Gregorian calendar.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Section 2
Possible Sections.Full Text: “easement” includes a right not arising from contract, by which one person is entitled to remove and appropriate for his own profit any part of the soil belonging to another.

Section Text: Nothing is an interruption within the meaning of this section, unless where there is an actual discontinuance of the possession or enjoyment by reason of an obstruction by the act of some person other than the claimant, and unless such obstruction is submitted to or acquiesced in for one year after the claimant has notice thereof and of the person making or authorising the same to be made.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: How long must an obstruction be acquiesced in to legally constitute an 'interruption' of an easement?

Rule.Section 26: This section defines an interruption as an obstruction that the claimant submits to for at least one year after notice.
Rule.Section 27: This provision excludes periods of life interests when calculating the total twenty-year easement period.
Rule.Section 28: This rule mandates the extinguishment of a right to property if a suit for possession is not filed in time.
Rule.Section 25: This section requires all instruments to be interpreted using the Gregorian calendar system.
Rule.Section 2: This section defines an easement as a right that can include taking soil for profit from another's land.

Application.Section 26: The Explanation specifically requires the obstruction to be submitted to for one year after notice to count as an interruption, confirming B.
Application.Section 27: Computational exclusions for life estates do not define the technical meaning of an 'interruption'.
Application.Section 28: Loss of title through limitation is distinct from the interruption of the prescriptive acquisition of an easement.
Application.Section 25: Standard calendar rules apply to the measurement of time but not the substantive definition of an obstruction's impact.
Application.Section 2: The definitional nature of an easement does not include the procedural rules for what breaks the continuity of use.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Limitation Act, 1908, section 26, Explanation, an interruption of an easement only occurs if the claimant submits to an obstruction for at least one year after having notice of it. Brief or non-acquiesced obstructions do not break the twenty-year prescriptive period. Therefore, (D) One year is correct.

Question:

Possible Sections.Full Text: “foreign country” means any country other than Bangladesh: “good faith”: nothing shall be deemed to be done in good faith which is not done with due care and attention.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Section 3

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Section 1

Section Text: Nothing in this Act shall apply to suits under the Divorce Act.

Rule.Section 29: This savings section explicitly states that the Act does not apply to suits under the Divorce Act.
Rule.Section 30-31: These sections have been repealed and no longer carry substantive provisions.
Rule.Section 2: This section provides definitions for terms like 'foreign country' and 'good faith'.
Rule.Section 3: This rule requires the dismissal of late suits, appeals, and applications generally.
Rule.Section 1: This section specifies the title and territorial extent of the Act.

Application.Section 29: Subsection 3 clearly states that nothing in the Limitation Act applies to suits under the Divorce Act, confirming B.
Application.Section 30-31: Repealed sections cannot provide current exemptions for other legal instruments.
Application.Section 2: Definitions of terms do not serve as an exclusion of entire Acts from the Limitation Act's scope.
Application.Section 3: The general mandate for dismissal is subject to the specific exclusions found in the savings clause.
Application.Section 1: Territorial extent specifies where the Act applies but does not list subject matter exemptions.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Limitation Act, 1908, section 29, Subsection (3), suits brought under the Divorce Act are entirely exempt from the provisions of the Limitation Act. Question:

Possible Sections.Full Text: All instruments shall, for the purposes of this Act, be deemed to be made with reference to the Gregorian calendar.

Possible Sections.Full Text: This Act may be called the Limitation Act, 1908. It extends to the whole of Bangladesh.

Section Text: “bond” includes any instrument whereby a person obliges himself to pay money to another, on condition that the obligation shall be void if a specified act is performed, or is not performed, as the case may be:

Rule.Section 2: Subsection 3 defines a bond as an instrument where an obligation to pay is voided by the performance or non-performance of an act.
Rule.Section 9: This section establishes the principle of continuous running of time once limitation has started.
Rule.Section 19: This rule computes a fresh limitation period from a written acknowledgment of liability.
Rule.Section 25: This provision mandates that all instruments must be interpreted using the Gregorian calendar.
Rule.Section 1: This section provides the basic title and territorial extent of the Act.

Application.Section 2: The definition explicitly includes instruments where the obligation is void if an act is performed or not performed, confirming option A.
Application.Section 9: Continuous running of time is a procedural rule that does not define specific legal instruments.
Application.Section 19: Acknowledgments extend time for existing obligations but do not define the nature of a bond.

Application.Section 1: Introductory details do not provide substantive definitions for commercial or legal instruments.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Limitation Act, 1908, section 2, Subsection (3), a "bond" is defined as an instrument where a person obliges themselves to pay money, but the obligation becomes void if a specified act is performed or not performed. Question:
Under Section 14 of the Limitation Act, 1908, for time to be excluded, the civil proceeding must be founded upon the ________.
(A) Same physical location
(B) Consent of the parties
(C) Same cause of action
(D) Original written contract

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Section 14
Possible Sections.Full Text: In computing the period of limitation prescribed for any suit, the time during which the plantiff has been prosecuting with due diligence another civil proceeding... shall be excluded, where the proceeding is founded upon the same cause of action.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Section 15
Possible Sections.Full Text: In computing the period of limitation prescribed for any suit or application for the execution of a decree, the institution or execution of which has been stayed by injunction or order.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Section 16
Possible Sections.Full Text: In computing the period of limitation prescribed for a suit for possession by a purchaser at a sale in execution of a decree, the time during which a proceeding to set aside the sale has been prosecuted shall be excluded.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Section 22
Possible Sections.Full Text: Where, after the institution of a suit, a new plaintiff or defendant is substituted or added, the suit shall, as regards him, be deemed to have been instituted when he was so made a party.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Section 12
Possible Sections.Full Text: In computing the period of limitation prescribed for any suit, appeal or application, the day from which such period is to be reckoned shall be excluded.

Section Text: In computing the period of limitation prescribed for any suit, the time during which the plantiff has been prosecuting with due diligence another civil proceeding... shall be excluded, where the proceeding is founded upon the same cause of action and is prosecuted in good faith in a Court which, from defect of jurisdiction, or other cause of a like nature, is unable to entertain it.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: What is the required legal nexus between two proceedings for the first to be excluded from the second's limitation period?

Rule.Section 14: This provision excludes time spent in an incompetent court if the former proceeding was based on the same cause of action.
Rule.Section 15: This rule excludes time from limitation when a suit has been suspended by a court injunction.
Rule.Section 16: This provision excludes time spent in proceedings to set aside an execution-sale for a purchaser.
Rule.Section 22: This section defines the institution date for parties added or substituted after the initial filing.
Rule.Section 12: This rule requires the exclusion of the day of reckoning from any limitation calculation.

Application.Section 14: The statute explicitly requires that the former proceeding must be founded on the 'same cause of action', confirming option A.
Application.Section 15: Injunction-based exclusions apply to the same suit, not necessarily requiring a comparison between two different ones.
Application.Section 16: Specific exclusions for purchasers relate to a single execution event, not the general concept of 'cause of action'.
Application.Section 22: Procedural timing for new parties is a matter of joinder, not the comparison of different causes of action.
Application.Section 12: Basic calculation rules apply to all suits regardless of their underlying legal basis.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Limitation Act, 1908, section 14, Subsection (1), for time spent in an incompetent court to be excluded from a new suit, the former proceeding must have been founded upon the same cause of action. This ensures the exclusion only applies to identical legal disputes. Therefore, (C) Same cause of action is correct.

Rule.Article 7: This article dictates the limitation window for recovering unpaid wages of domestic or manual workers.

Rule.Article 113: This article sets the general three-year limit for suits seeking the specific performance of contracts.

Question:

IRAC_Reasoning.Application: The limitation clock stops at the moment of application, rather than when the plaint is eventually registered.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, the Explanation to Section 3 states that a pauper's suit is instituted when the application for leave to sue as a pauper is made. Therefore, (B) The application for leave to sue as a pauper is made is correct.

Question:

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 6, Subsection (2) mandates that if two disabilities are present, the suit may be filed after both have ceased. Therefore, (A) After both disabilities have ceased is correct.

Question:

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, the proviso to Section 9 states that if a debtor administers a creditor's estate, the limitation period for the debt is suspended during that administration. Therefore, (B) Suspended while the administration continues is correct.

Question:

IRAC_Reasoning.Application: If a suit was filed on Jan 1 and dismissed for lack of jurisdiction on Jan 10, all 10 days are excluded from the new suit's limitation.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, explanation I of Section 14 states that both the day of institution and the day the proceedings ended must be counted in the excluded period. Therefore, (B) Both the day of institution and the day the proceedings ended is correct.

Question:

IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: Section 24 computes the period from the moment the specific injury actually results.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: If a person digs a hole on their land (not actionable) and the neighbor's wall subsides a year later, limitation starts from the subsidence.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 24 states that for acts not actionable per se, limitation is computed from the time the injury results. Therefore, (A) The time when the injury results is correct.

Question:
Under Section 26 of The Penal Code, 1860, the right to an easement becomes 'absolute and indefeasible' after peaceable enjoyment for:
(A) 12 years
(B) 30 years
(C) 60 years
(D) 20 years

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Penal Code, 1860, Section 26
Possible Sections.Full Text: Subsection (1) — ...enjoyed therewith as an easement, and as of right, without interruption, and for twenty years...

Section Text: peaceably and openly enjoyed by any person claiming title thereto as an easement and as of right without interruption, and for twenty years.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: What is the standard period for acquiring a prescriptive easement right?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: Section 26(1) sets the period at twenty years of peaceable, open, and uninterrupted enjoyment.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: Consistent use of a pathway over private land for 20 years establishes a permanent legal right.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 26(1) specifies twenty years of enjoyment for an easement right to become absolute. Therefore, (D) 20 years is correct.

Question:
Regarding Section 27 of The Penal Code, 1860, who can resist a claim to an easement within three years after the determination of a life interest?
(A) The District Collector
(B) The person entitled to the land on determination (reversioner)
(C) Any neighbor
(D) The life tenant

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Penal Code, 1860, Section 27
Possible Sections.Full Text: ...in case the claim is, within three years next after the determination of such interest or term, resisted by the person entitled, on such determination, to the said land or water.

Section Text: resisted by the person entitled, on such determination, to the said land or water.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Who has the right to exclude time of enjoyment against an easement claim after a life interest ends?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: Section 27 allows the reversioner (person entitled after the life interest) to resist the claim.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: The law protects the ultimate owner from easements that were developing while someone else (a life tenant) held the land.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 27 states that the resistance must come from the person entitled to the land upon the determination of the interest. Therefore, (B) The person entitled to the land on determination (reversioner) is correct.

Question:
According to Section 29, the provisions of Sections 26 and 27 do NOT apply to territories where which Act extends?
(A) The Transfer of Property Act
(B) The Easements Act, 1882
(C) The Contract Act, 1872
(D) The Evidence Act, 1872

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Penal Code, 1860, Section 29
Possible Sections.Full Text: Subsection (4) — Sections 26 and 27... shall not apply to cases arising in territories to which the Easements Act, 1882, may... extend.

Section Text: Sections 26 and 27 and the definition of “easement” in section 2 shall not apply to cases arising in territories to which the Easements Act, 1882, may... extend.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Where do the Limitation Act's easement provisions cease to apply?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: Section 29(4) states these sections do not apply in territories governed by the Easements Act, 1882.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: To avoid statutory conflict, the specific Easements Act takes precedence in those regions.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 29(4) excludes the application of Sections 26 and 27 in territories covered by the Easements Act, 1882. Therefore, (B) The Easements Act, 1882 is correct.

Question:
In computing the period for a suit against a company being wound up, institution occurs when:
(A) The petition for winding up is filed
(B) The claimant first sends in his claim to the official liquidator
(C) The liquidator is appointed
(D) The assets are distributed

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Penal Code, 1860, Section 3
Possible Sections.Full Text: A suit is instituted... in the case of a claim against a company... when the claimant first sends in his claim to the official liquidator.

Section Text: in the case of a claim against a company which is being wound up by the Court, when the claimant first sends in his claim to the official liquidator.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: When is a claim against a company in liquidation deemed instituted?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: The Explanation to Section 3 identifies the sending of the claim to the official liquidator as the moment of institution.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: Limitation is arrested once the formal claim is submitted to the person managing the winding-up.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, the Explanation to Section 3 defines institution for companies in liquidation as the date the claim is sent to the official liquidator. Therefore, (B) The claimant first sends in his claim to the official liquidator is correct.

Question:
Under Section 11 of The Penal Code, 1860, local suits on foreign contracts are subject to:
(A) The rules of limitation contained in this Act
(B) International law
(C) Foreign limitation laws only
(D) No limitation whatever

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Penal Code, 1860, Section 11
Possible Sections.Full Text: Subsection (1) — Suits instituted in Bangladesh on contracts entered into in a foreign country are subject to the rules of limitation contained in this Act.

Section Text: Suits instituted in Bangladesh on contracts entered into in a foreign country are subject to the rules of limitation contained in this Act.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Which limitation laws apply to a suit filed in Bangladesh regarding a contract made abroad?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: Section 11(1) mandates the application of the local Limitation Act, 1908.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: Even if a contract was signed in the UK, a suit filed in a Dhaka court must adhere to the time limits of the Bangladesh statute.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 11(1) subjects foreign contracts sued upon locally to the Bangladesh Limitation Act. Therefore, (A) The rules of limitation contained in this Act is correct.

Question:
Under Section 10 of The Penal Code, 1860, property comprised in a Hindu, Muslim or Buddhist religious or charitable endowment is deemed to be:
(A) Private property of the manager
(B) Property vested in trust for a specific purpose
(C) Property subject to a 12-year limitation period
(D) Government property

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Penal Code, 1860, Section 10
Possible Sections.Full Text: Notwithstanding anything hereinbefore contained, no suit against a person in whom property has become vested in trust for any specific purpose... shall be barred by any length of time. For the purposes of this section any property comprised in a Hindu, Muslim or Buddhist religious or charitable endowment shall be deemed to be property vested in trust for a specific purpose...

Section Text: For the purposes of this section any property comprised in a Hindu, Muslim or Buddhist religious or charitable endowment shall be deemed to be property vested in trust for a specific purpose, and the manager of any such property shall be deemed to be the trustee thereof.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: How does the Act categorize religious or charitable endowments for limitation purposes?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: Under Section 10, property in Hindu, Muslim, or Buddhist endowments is deemed vested in trust for a specific purpose, and its manager is deemed a trustee.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: Because these are deemed 'trusts for a specific purpose,' suits to follow such property are not barred by any length of time.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 10 explicitly deems property in Hindu, Muslim, or Buddhist religious or charitable endowments as property vested in trust for a specific purpose. Therefore, (B) Property vested in trust for a specific purpose is correct.

Question:
According to Section 11 of The Penal Code, 1860, a foreign rule of limitation shall NOT be a defence to a suit in Bangladesh unless the rule has:
(A) Been pleaded by both the plaintiff and defendant
(B) Extinguished the contract and the parties were domiciled in that country
(C) Prescribed a period longer than the local Act
(D) Been ratified by the Bangladesh Government

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Penal Code, 1860, Section 11
Possible Sections.Full Text: No foreign rule of limitation shall be a defence... unless the rule has extinguished the contract and the parties were domiciled in such country during the period prescribed by such rule.

Section Text: No foreign rule of limitation shall be a defence to a suit instituted in Bangladesh on a contract entered into in a foreign country, unless the rule has extinguished the contract and the parties were domiciled in such country during the period prescribed by such rule.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Under what conditions can a foreign limitation law be used as a defense in Bangladesh?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: Section 11(2) states that a foreign rule is only a defense if it extinguished the contract and the parties were domiciled in that country during the prescribed period.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: If the foreign law merely bars the remedy but doesn't extinguish the right, it cannot be used as a defense locally.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 11(2) provides that a foreign rule of limitation is only a defense if it extinguished the contract and the parties were domiciled in that foreign country. Therefore, (B) Extinguished the contract and the parties were domiciled in that country is correct.

Question:
Under Section 12 of The Penal Code, 1860, when a decree is appealed from, what else shall be excluded besides the time for obtaining a copy of the decree?
(A) The time requisite for obtaining a copy of the judgment
(B) The time during which the court was on vacation
(C) The time spent in settlement negotiations
(D) The time required to travel to the court

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Penal Code, 1860, Section 12
Possible Sections.Full Text: Subsection (3) — Where a decree is appealed from or sought to be reviewed, the time requisite for obtaining a copy of the judgment on which it is founded shall also be excluded.

Section Text: Where a decree is appealed from or sought to be reviewed, the time requisite for obtaining a copy of the judgment on which it is founded shall also be excluded.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: What is excluded when appealing a decree founded on a judgment?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: Section 12(3) mandates that the time spent obtaining a copy of the judgment must also be excluded.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: An appellant is entitled to exclude time for both the decree and the underlying judgment.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 12(3) explicitly excludes the time required to obtain a copy of the judgment on which the decree is founded. Therefore, (A) The time requisite for obtaining a copy of the judgment is correct.

Question:
In computing limitation for a suit, Section 13 of The Penal Code, 1860 excludes the time during which the defendant has been absent from:
(A) The Asian continent
(B) Their permanent residence
(C) The jurisdiction of the District Court
(D) Bangladesh and territories under Government administration

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Penal Code, 1860, Section 13
Possible Sections.Full Text: In computing the period... the time during which the defendant has been absent from Bangladesh... shall be excluded.

Section Text: In computing the period of limitation prescribed for any suit, the time during which the defendant has been absent from Bangladesh and from the territories beyond Bangladesh under the administration of the Government shall be excluded.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: How does a defendant's absence from the country affect limitation?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: Section 13 excludes the time a defendant is absent from Bangladesh and government-administered territories.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: If a defendant is abroad for 2 years, those 2 years are subtracted from the limitation calculation.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 13 mandates the exclusion of time when the defendant is absent from Bangladesh and its administered territories. Therefore, (D) Bangladesh and territories under Government administration is correct.

Question:
Under Section 14 of The Penal Code, 1860, for the purpose of excluding time, a plaintiff or applicant resisting an appeal shall be deemed to be:
(A) Acting in bad faith
(B) In default of appearance
(C) Prosecuting a proceeding
(D) Delaying the proceedings

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Penal Code, 1860, Section 14
Possible Sections.Full Text: Explanation II—For the purposes of this section, a plaintiff or an applicant resisting an appeal shall be deemed to be prosecuting a proceeding.

Section Text: Explanation II—For the purposes of this section, a plaintiff or an applicant resisting an appeal shall be deemed to be prosecuting a proceeding.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Does resisting an appeal count as 'prosecuting a proceeding' for time exclusion?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: Under Explanation II of Section 14, resisting an appeal is legally deemed as prosecuting a proceeding.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: This allows a party to claim the exclusion of time even if they were the respondent in the previous stage.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, explanation II of Section 14 states that resisting an appeal is deemed as prosecuting a proceeding. Therefore, (C) Prosecuting a proceeding is correct.

Question:
When a suit is stayed by an injunction, Section 15(1) of The Penal Code, 1860 excludes the day the injunction was issued and the day it was:
(A) Served on the defendant
(B) Reported in the news
(C) Argued in court
(D) Withdrawn

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Penal Code, 1860, Section 15
Possible Sections.Full Text: Subsection (1) — ...the time of the continuance of the injunction or order, the day on which it was issued or made, and the day on which it was withdrawn, shall be excluded.

Section Text: the day on which it was issued or made, and the day on which it was withdrawn, shall be excluded.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Which specific terminal days are excluded when a suit is stayed by an injunction?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: Section 15(1) excludes the continuance of the injunction, the day it was issued/made, and the day it was withdrawn.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: Both the starting and ending days of the injunction period are subtracted from the limitation calculation.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 15(1) provides for the exclusion of the day the injunction was issued and the day it was withdrawn. Therefore, (D) Withdrawn is correct.

Question:
Section 16 of The Penal Code, 1860 excludes time for a suit for possession by a purchaser at an execution-sale while a proceeding to ________ the sale is pending.
(A) Confirm
(B) Publicize
(C) Set aside
(D) Register

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Penal Code, 1860, Section 16
Possible Sections.Full Text: In computing the period... for a suit for possession by a purchaser... the time during which a proceeding to set aside the sale has been prosecuted shall be excluded.

Section Text: In computing the period of limitation prescribed for a suit for possession by a purchaser at a sale in execution of a decree, the time during which a proceeding to set aside the sale has been prosecuted shall be excluded.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: What period is excluded for a purchaser at an execution sale?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: Section 16 excludes time while a proceeding to 'set aside' the sale is being prosecuted.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: If the sale is challenged, the purchaser’s limitation for seeking possession is paused until the challenge ends.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 16 specifically excludes the time during which a proceeding to set aside the sale is pending. Therefore, (C) Set aside is correct.

Question:
Under Section 17 of The Penal Code, 1860, if a person against whom a right to sue would have accrued dies before it accrues, time runs from when there is a:
(A) Order from the Supreme Court
(B) Official gazette notification
(C) Legal representative against whom the plaintiff may sue
(D) Will found in the deceased’s house

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Penal Code, 1860, Section 17
Possible Sections.Full Text: Subsection (2) — ...the period of limitation shall be computed from the time when there is a legal representative of the deceased against whom the plaintiff may institute or make such suit or application.

Section Text: Where person against whom, if he were living, a right to institute a suit or make an application would have accrued dies before the right accrues, the period of limitation shall be computed from the time when there is a legal representative of the deceased against whom the plaintiff may institute or make such suit or application.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: When does limitation start if the potential defendant dies before the cause of action arises?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: Under Section 17(2), time is computed from when a legal representative exists who can be sued.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: Limitation cannot run until there is a party capable of being sued (defendant).
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 17(2) provides that limitation runs once a legal representative exists against whom the suit may be instituted. Therefore, (C) Legal representative against whom the plaintiff may sue is correct.

Question:
In cases of fraud under Section 18 of The Penal Code, 1860, if a necessary document has been concealed, the time is computed from when the person first:
(A) Suspected the document existed
(B) Had the means of producing it or compelling its production
(C) Lost the original right to sue
(D) Filed a police report

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Penal Code, 1860, Section 18
Possible Sections.Full Text: Where any person... by means of fraud, been kept from the knowledge... or where any document necessary to establish such right has been fraudulently concealed... shall be computed... in the case of the concealed document, when he first had the means of producing it or compelling its production.

Section Text: ...in the case of the concealed document, when he first had the means of producing it or compelling its production.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: How is limitation affected by the fraudulent concealment of a necessary document?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: Section 18 states that for a concealed document, time is computed from when the person had the means of producing or compelling it.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: Fraudulent concealment pauses the clock until the document is practically available to the victim.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 18 provides that time runs from when the affected person first has the means of producing or compelling the production of the concealed document. Therefore, (B) Had the means of producing it or compelling its production is correct.

Question:
Under Section 19 of The Penal Code, 1860, if an acknowledgment is signed by an 'agent duly authorized', it means it must be signed:
(A) Either personally or by a duly authorized agent
(B) Personally by the party only
(C) By the plaintiff’s lawyer
(D) By a court officer

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Penal Code, 1860, Section 19
Possible Sections.Full Text: Explanation II—For the purposes of this section, “signed” means signed either personally or by an agent duly authorized in this behalf.

Section Text: Explanation II—For the purposes of this section, “signed” means signed either personally or by an agent duly authorized in this behalf.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Who can sign an acknowledgment for it to be valid under Section 19?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: Explanation II of Section 19 defines 'signed' as signed personally or by a duly authorized agent.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: An acknowledgment signed by an agent is as legally binding as one signed by the party themselves.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, explanation II of Section 19 clarifies that 'signed' includes signatures by the party or their authorized agent. Therefore, (A) Either personally or by a duly authorized agent is correct.

Question:
Regarding Section 20 of The Penal Code, 1860, a payment of interest made before the 1st day of January, 1928, differs from later payments because it does NOT require:
(A) Consent of the Court
(B) Two witnesses
(C) Payment in local currency
(D) A written acknowledgment of the payment

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Penal Code, 1860, Section 20
Possible Sections.Full Text: Subsection (1) — ...Provided that, save in the case of a payment of interest made before the 1st day of January, 1928, an acknowledgment of the payment appears in the handwriting... or signed...

Section Text: Provided that, save in the case of a payment of interest made before the 1st day of January, 1928, an acknowledgment of the payment appears in the handwriting of, or in a writing signed by the person making the payment.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: What is the specific requirement for acknowledging interest payments after Jan 1, 1928?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: Section 20(1) mandates that for payments after 1928, an acknowledgment must be in handwriting or signed.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: Payments before 1928 are excepted from this writing requirement.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, the proviso in Section 20(1) states that payments after Jan 1, 1928, must be acknowledged in writing or signed, unlike those made before that date. Therefore, (D) A written acknowledgment of the payment is correct.

Question:
Under Section 21(3)(a) of The Penal Code, 1860, a valid acknowledgment signed by which of the following is binding against a reversioner under Hindu law?
(A) A widow or other limited owner
(B) Any family member
(C) The eldest son only
(D) A neighbor of the family

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Penal Code, 1860, Section 21
Possible Sections.Full Text: Subsection (3)(a) — an acknowledgment signed... by... any widow or other limited owner... shall be a valid acknowledgment... as against a reversioner succeeding to such liability.

Section Text: an acknowledgment signed, or a payment made... by... any widow or other limited owner of property who is governed by the Hindu law, shall be a valid acknowledgment or payment... as against a reversioner succeeding to such liability.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Can a Hindu widow's acknowledgment bind a reversioner?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: Section 21(3)(a) validates acknowledgments by a widow or limited owner against a reversioner.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: This ensures liability recognized by a limited owner remains valid for the next successor (reversioner).
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 21(3)(a) states that an acknowledgment by a widow or limited owner is valid against a reversioner. Therefore, (A) A widow or other limited owner is correct.

Question:
Under Section 22(1) of The Penal Code, 1860, if a new defendant is substituted, the suit is deemed instituted against them:
(A) When they received the summons
(B) When they were made a party
(C) When they filed their written statement
(D) On the date the original suit was filed

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Penal Code, 1860, Section 22
Possible Sections.Full Text: Subsection (1) — ...the suit shall, as regards him, be deemed to have been instituted when he was so made a party.

Section Text: Where, after the institution of a suit, a new plaintiff or defendant is substituted or added, the suit shall, as regards him, be deemed to have been instituted when he was so made a party.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: When is a suit deemed instituted for a new party?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: Section 22(1) states institution for a new party happens when they are officially made a party to the suit.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: Limitation for the new party is calculated from the date of their joinder, not the original filing date.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 22(1) provides that a suit is deemed instituted for a new party when they were so made a party. Therefore, (B) When they were made a party is correct.

Question:
In continuing breaches of contract under Section 23 of The Penal Code, 1860, a fresh period of limitation begins to run at:
(A) Every moment of the time during which the breach continues
(B) The end of the contract term
(C) The date the breach is first discovered
(D) The end of each month

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Penal Code, 1860, Section 23
Possible Sections.Full Text: In the case of a continuing breach... a fresh period of limitation begins to run at every moment... during which the breach... continues.

Section Text: In the case of a continuing breach of contract... a fresh period of limitation begins to run at every moment of the time during which the breach or the wrong, as the case may be, continues.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: How does limitation run for a continuing breach of contract?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: Section 23 dictates that a fresh period begins at 'every moment' the breach persists.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: For as long as the wrong continues, the limitation period constantly restarts.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 23 provides that for a continuing breach, a fresh period of limitation runs at every moment of the continuance of the breach. Therefore, (A) Every moment of the time during which the breach continues is correct.

Question:
Under Section 24 of The Penal Code, 1860, for acts not actionable without special damage, limitation is computed from when the:
(A) Act is performed
(B) Specific damage is paid
(C) Defendant is notified
(D) Injury results

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Penal Code, 1860, Section 24
Possible Sections.Full Text: ...the period of limitation shall be computed from the time when the injury results.

Section Text: In the case of a suit for compensation for an act which does not give rise to a cause of action unless some specific injury actually results therefrom, the period of limitation shall be computed from the time when the injury results.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: When does limitation start for acts that require special damage to be actionable?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: Section 24 states that limitation is computed from when the 'injury results'.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: The cause of action only matures when the injury occurs, thus the clock starts then.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 24 mandates that limitation for such acts is computed from the time when the injury results. Therefore, (D) Injury results is correct.

Question:
According to Section 25 of The Penal Code, 1860, a promissory note bearing a Native date is deemed to be made with reference to the:
(A) Calendar of the defendant’s choice
(B) Bengali calendar
(C) Islamic calendar
(D) Gregorian calendar

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Penal Code, 1860, Section 25
Possible Sections.Full Text: All instruments shall... be deemed to be made with reference to the Gregorian calendar.

Section Text: All instruments shall, for the purposes of this Act, be deemed to be made with reference to the Gregorian calendar. Illustration (a): A Hindu makes a promissory note bearing a Native date only... computed according to the Gregorian calendar.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Which calendar is used to interpret dates in instruments for limitation purposes?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: Section 25 mandates that all instruments are deemed made with reference to the Gregorian calendar.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: Native dates must be converted to the Gregorian equivalent to calculate the limitation period.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 25 and its illustration (a) confirm that instruments are deemed to refer to the Gregorian calendar. Therefore, (D) is correct.

Question:
Under Section 26(2) of The Penal Code, 1860, an easement right over property belonging to the Government requires enjoyment for:
(A) 20 years
(B) Indefinite period
(C) 60 years
(D) 12 years

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Penal Code, 1860, Section 26
Possible Sections.Full Text: Subsection (2) — Where the property... belongs to the Government... sixty years were substituted.

Section Text: Where the property over which a right is claimed under sub-section (1) belongs to the Government, that sub-section shall be read as if for the words “twenty years” the words “sixty years” were substituted.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: How long must one enjoy an easement against the Government?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: Section 26(2) substitutes 'sixty years' for 'twenty years' if the property belongs to the Government.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: Acquiring rights against the State requires a significantly longer duration than against private individuals.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 26(2) explicitly requires sixty years of enjoyment for easement rights over Government property. Therefore, (C) 60 years is correct.

Question:
Under Section 28 of The Penal Code, 1860, once the limitation period for instituting a suit for possession of property expires, the right to such property shall be:
(A) Suspended
(B) Renewed automatically
(C) Transferred to the State
(D) Extinguished

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Penal Code, 1860, Section 28
Possible Sections.Full Text: At the determination of the period... for instituting a suit for possession... his right to such property shall be extinguished.

Section Text: At the determination of the period hereby limited to any person for instituting a suit for possession of any property, his right to such property shall be extinguished.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: What happens to a person’s right to property when they fail to sue for possession in time?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: Section 28 states that the right to such property is 'extinguished' at the end of the limitation period.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: Unlike other rights where only the remedy is barred, here the substantive right itself is destroyed.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 28 explicitly provides that the right to property is extinguished upon the expiration of the limitation period for a possession suit. Therefore, (D) Extinguished is correct.

Question:
According to Section 29, when a special law prescribes a different period of limitation, which section of the general Act still applies?
(A) Sections 26 and 27
(B) None of the sections
(C) Section 3 and portions of Section 4, 9-18, 22
(D) Section 3 only

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Penal Code, 1860, Section 29
Possible Sections.Full Text: Subsection (2) — ...the provision of section 3 shall apply... and for the purpose of determining any period... section 4, sections 9 to 18, and section 22 shall apply...

Section Text: the provision of section 3 shall apply... the provisions contained in section 4, sections 9 to 18, and section 22 shall apply only in so far as, and to the extent to which, they are not expressly excluded by such special law.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Which general provisions apply to special laws?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: Section 29(2) applies Section 3 and allows Sections 4, 9-18, and 22 to apply unless expressly excluded by the special law.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: General principles of computation (like court closures) apply to special laws unless the special law says otherwise.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 29(2) applies Section 3 and sections 4, 9-18, and 22 to special laws unless they are expressly excluded. Therefore, (C) Section 3 and portions of Section 4, 9-18, 22 is correct.

Question:
Under the Specific Relief Act, 1877, 'obligation' includes —
(A) Every moral duty
(B) Duties involving trust property
(C) Every duty enforceable by law
(D) Only contractual duties

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Specific Relief Act, 1877, Section 1
Possible Sections.Full Text: This Act may be called the Specific Relief Act, 1877. It extends to the whole of Bangladesh. And it shall come into force on the first day of May, 1877.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Specific Relief Act, 1877, Section 3
Possible Sections.Full Text: In this Act, unless there be something repugnant in the subject or context,- "obligation" includes every duty enforceable by law: "trust" includes every species of express, implied, or constructive fiduciary ownership: "trustee" includes every person holding, expressly, by implication, or constructively, a fiduciary character:

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Specific Relief Act, 1877, Section 4
Possible Sections.Full Text: Except where it is herein otherwise expressly enacted, nothing in this Act shall be deemed- (a) to give any right to relief in respect of any agreement which is not a contract; (b) to deprive any person of any right to relief, other than specific performance, which he may have under any contract; or (c) to affect the operation of the Registration Act, 1908 on documents.

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Specific Relief Act, 1877, Section 11

Application.Section 12: While Section 12 gives general power, Section 13 specifically addresses the destruction of subject matter.

Section Text: Temporary injunctions are such as are to continue until a specified time, or until the further order of the Court. They may be granted at any period of a suit, and are regulated by the Code of Civil Procedure. A perpetual injunction can only be granted by the decree made at the hearing and upon the merits of the suit: the defendant is thereby perpetually enjoined from the assertion of a right, or from the commission of an act, which would be contrary to the rights of the plaintiff.

Application.Section 52: General granting power for injunctions is provided here but not the detailed procedural regulation.
Application.Section 53: The statutory language specifically names the 'Code of Civil Procedure' as the regulator for temporary injunctions, confirming option B.
Application.Section 55: Mandatory injunctions focus on substantive requirements rather than the procedural code for temporary orders.

IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Specific Relief Act, 1877, section 53, temporary injunctions are specifically defined as being regulated by the Code of Civil Procedure.
